abdelrahman_amr.research_focus: Dr. Abd-Elrahman is an Associate Professor of Geomatics whose research focuses on remote sensing and geospatial analysis applications in precision agriculture and natural resource management. He utilizes multi-spectral and hyperspectral images acquired from satellite, airborne, and small Unmanned Aircraft Systems (sUAS) as well as LiDAR datasets. He uses machine learning algorithms, including deep learning networks (CNN, FCN, Deeplab) to analyze high spatial resolution sUAS images for wetland classification, vegetation analysis, and ecosystem monitoring.

acomb_glenn.research_focus: Glenn Acomb's research focuses on green roof technology and sustainable stormwater management in sub-tropical and hurricane-prone climates. Current research activities involve developing new selections of green roof media and plant materials for sub-tropical projects, studying wind uplift on green roofs for hurricane regions' building codes, and evaluating the performance of design and plant selections in Low Impact Development (LID) stormwater devices in Florida.

adams_damian.name: Damian C. Adams
adams_damian.academic_unit: School of Forest, Fisheries & Geomatics Sciences; Food and Resource Economics Department
education: Ph.D. Food and Resource Economics, University of Florida (2007)

J.D. Law, University of Florida (2001)
adams_damian.research_focus: Dr. Adams is a Professor of Natural Resource Economics and Policy and serves as Associate Dean for Research within UF/IFAS. His research centers on non-market valuation of ecosystem goods and services, particularly related to forests and natural resources. He studies how people value ecosystem services, how preferences and attitudes affect those values, and how this information can inform policy decisions. His work uses survey-based data, econometric and bioeconomic modeling, and non-market valuation methods to address topics such as invasive species management, conservation policy, carbon sequestration, water resources, and economic valuation of natural resources.

B.S. Chemical Engineering, Penn State University (1996)
adams_peter.research_focus: Dr. Adams is a Professor of Geological Sciences specializing in quantitative geomorphology and coastal processes. His research focuses on understanding the geomorphic evolution of landscapes resulting from the interaction of coastal, fluvial, and tectonic processes. He leads the UF Geomorphology Lab, conducting field measurements and computer simulations to understand relationships between sediment transport, shoreline change, and morphological evolution. Primary methods include real-time instrumentation to examine physical processes, digital elevation analyses to examine landscape form, and numerical modeling to link process and form together. Current field sites include the Florida Atlantic coast, the Southern California bight, and the Kenai Peninsula in South Central Alaska.

agehara_shinsuke.research_focus: Dr. Agehara is an Associate Professor of Horticulture who leads the Agehara Lab at GCREC. His research and extension program focuses on developing vegetable and fruit production practices to improve productivity, product quality, stress tolerance, and resource use efficiency—especially under environmental stresses such as heat, water, and nutrient limitations. The program covers Florida's major crops including tomato, strawberry, blueberry, bell pepper, and cucurbits, as well as specialty crops such as blackberry, pomegranate, hops, hemp, and artichoke. Core research themes include plant adaptive responses to environmental changes and abiotic stresses, irrigation and fertilization practices for sustainability, cultural management and protected agriculture, and integration of advanced techniques such as remote sensing, image-based diagnostics, and crop modeling.

albertin_andrea.research_focus: Dr. Albertin is a Regional Specialized Agent in Water Resources, leading water-related extension education across the Florida Panhandle. Her extension programs focus on water quality protection by reducing point and non-point source pollution, promotion of Florida-Friendly Landscaping principles, septic system management, water conservation, and increasing use-efficiency in both agricultural and urban sectors. She is a regional expert in water resources issues including private well systems, drinking water quality, watershed pollution prevention, and agricultural best management practices.

allen_micheal.name: Micheal S. Allen
allen_micheal.academic_unit: School of Forest, Fisheries and Geomatics Sciences; Fisheries and Aquatic Sciences
education: Ph.D. Fisheries Management and Statistics
allen_micheal.research_focus: Dr. Allen is a Professor of Fisheries and Aquatic Sciences and Director of the UF/IFAS Nature Coast Biological Station (NCBS) in Cedar Key, FL. His research program evaluates fish population and fish community responses to changes in fishing mortality, habitat, and species interactions. He and his students evaluate the efficacy of various management strategies for improving and sustaining fisheries. Research areas include fish population dynamics and ecology, sustainable harvest approaches, habitat requirements and ecological responses for both freshwater and coastal fisheries, and effects of fishing mortality, invasive species, and environmental change on fish communities. He uses field studies, modeling techniques, and applied research to inform fisheries conservation and management in Florida and beyond.

keywords: Fisheries science, fish population dynamics, recreational fisheries management, habitat restoration, coastal ecology, fish community responses, simulation modelling, living shorelines.

allen_mike.research_focus: Mike Allen’s research program centres on fish population dynamics and fish community ecology in response to fishing pressure, habitat change, and species interactions. He employs a blend of empirical field studies, pond experiments, and computer simulation modelling to assess how various management strategies affect recreational fisheries. His current projects include evaluating coastal habitat restoration (such as living shorelines and reef-ball installations along the Cedar Key coastline) to support marine fish communities and prevent erosion.

almeydazambrano_angelica.research_focus: Dr. Almeyda Zambrano is Research Faculty at the UF Center for Latin American Studies and co-director of the Spatial Ecology and Conservation (SPEC) Lab. Her research centers on human-environment interactions, focusing on social and ecological feedback loops with consideration to sustainability. She examines land use and land cover change dynamics in the tropics and its relation to economic activities, with emphasis on nature tourism as a development and conservation strategy. Her scholarship integrates social and ecological data using geospatial analysis to understand deforestation, forest dynamics, ecosystem services, and socio-environmental drivers of change in Amazonia and other Neotropical landscapes.

altieri_andrew.research_focus: Dr. Altieri is an Associate Professor in Environmental Engineering Sciences and Interim Director at the Center for Coastal Solutions, where he leads the nature-based solutions program. His research examines how global drivers including habitat degradation, species invasions, extinctions, and overexploitation have cascading effects on biodiversity and emergent properties of coastal ecosystems. He employs an interdisciplinary combination of field experiments, large-scale observations, historical reconstruction, and ecophysiology techniques integrating from organism to seascape. Much of his current research focuses on ocean deoxygenation and how it affects habitat-forming species including corals, examining how dissolved oxygen depletion causes hypoxia-induced stress and mortality for scleractinian corals.

His program focuses on the design, development, and testing of sensors and control systems for optimal management of inputs, resources, and products. Research areas include mechanical harvesting of specialty crops, mechatronics, artificial intelligence, machine vision, precision agriculture, smart machines (smart spraying technologies), UAVs, and machine systems. His team develops AI-based systems to detect, distinguish, and categorize agricultural objects including citrus trees, fruit, flowers, weeds, and pests.

angelo_mary.research_focus: Professor Angelo is an Emeritus Samuel T. Dell Professor and Emeritus Professor of Law at the University of Florida Levin College of Law. Her scholarship draws on her background in biological sciences coupled with experience as an environmental lawyer. Her research explores the intersection of law and science, particularly focusing on how ecological sciences are used in, and can inform, environmental law. Areas of focus include pesticide law, endangered species law, water and wetlands law, sustainable agriculture, and regulation of genetically modified organisms.

His work bridges legal education and practical environmental law, focusing on domestic and international environmental law and policy, water law, coastal and marine law, and protection of biological diversity. He has worked extensively in Florida as well as in Latin America and the Caribbean, having taught and worked in 30+ countries. He is fluent in Spanish, supporting his extensive international engagement.

annable_michael.research_focus: Dr. Annable is a Professor in the Department of Environmental Engineering Sciences at the Herbert Wertheim College of Engineering, University of Florida. His research expertise centers on hydrology, water quality, and subsurface environmental processes, particularly physical-chemical processes for field-scale application of innovative subsurface remediation technologies, innovative measurement methods for contaminant and water flux in hydrologic systems, and development of passive flux meters and techniques for measuring interfaces in porous media. His work also addresses humid, shallow water-table environments such as the flatwoods of the southeastern United States, where significant interaction between surface water and groundwater occurs.

asche_frank.research_focus: Dr. Asche is a Professor of Natural Resource Economics in Fisheries and Aquatic Sciences at UF/IFAS and a Florida Sea Grant Affiliate Faculty member. He is one of the most cited economists globally in fisheries and aquaculture, with research covering economic analysis of virtually all aspects of the seafood system from production to final consumer. His work examines interactions between fisheries, aquaculture, and ecosystems, the impact of new supply chains on production systems and markets, and the effect of consumer trends on market strategies. He has empirically proven that seafood markets are globally integrated and established methods now standard in price transmission and market integration analysis.

atkinson_michelle.research_focus: Michelle Atkinson provides community outreach, education, and research-based programming related to environmental horticulture, sustainable landscaping practices, and water resource protection. She develops and delivers workshops and training for pest control professionals, lawn and landscape industries, golf courses, homeowners, and community groups. Her program areas include landscape management, urban and community pest management, and pesticide safety training and testing. She oversees a residential Mobile Irrigation Lab focused on encouraging outdoor water conservation and best management practices in the landscape. Michelle works closely with local stakeholders, homeowner associations, and professionals to implement science-based environmental horticulture solutions that protect water quality and conserve vital resources. She has co-authored UF/IFAS publications on horticulture and stormwater pond management.

bakeriii_terrell.research_focus: Dr. Terrell T. "Red" Baker III is the Director and Professor of the School of Forest Resources and Conservation (SFRC) at the University of Florida. He leads the interdisciplinary academic and outreach mission across forestry, fisheries, and geomatics sciences, serving faculty, staff, and students across undergraduate, graduate, and extension programs. His research expertise spans forest ecology, streamside management zones, watershed processes, fire ecology, and natural resource conflict mediation. Before joining UF, Dr. Baker served as Chair of the Department of Forestry at the University of Kentucky, where he was the James Graham Brown Endowed Professor of Forestry. He also spent many years as a Riparian (Streamside) Specialist at New Mexico State University, balancing research and extension in riverine and forest ecosystems.

baker_shirley.research_focus: Dr. Shirley M. Baker is a Professor of Molluscan Biology and Aquaculture in the Fisheries and Aquatic Sciences Program at UF. Her research bridges physiology, ecology, and aquaculture of marine invertebrates, especially shellfish. She focuses on understanding shellfish (e.g., hard clams, oysters) physiological responses to environmental factors, including the effects of salinity, temperature, water quality, and eutrophication on shellfish performance and ecosystem services. Her work includes developing models to predict impacts of environmental parameters on shellfish productivity and studying ecosystem services provided by shellfish, including nutrient cycling and water quality improvement. Her extension work supports the shellfish and aquaculture industries, citizens, and stakeholders throughout Florida by identifying industry needs, conducting applied research, and providing sustainable solutions.

baker_tracie.research_focus: Dr. Baker is an environmental toxicologist and director of the WATER (Warrior Aquatic, Translational, and Environmental Research) Laboratory. Her research focuses on One Health approaches evaluating developmentally-based and transgenerational, environmentally-induced disease from endocrine disrupting chemicals and contaminants of emerging concern including microplastics, PFAS, pharmaceuticals, pesticides, and personal care products.

balachandar_sivaramakrishnam.research_focus: Dr. Sivaramakrishnan "Bala" Balachandar is a globally recognized fluid mechanist and Distinguished Professor at the University of Florida. His research centers on fundamental and computational aspects of fluid mechanics, with particular strengths in turbulent flows and transition theory, multiphase flows (interactions between fluids and particles), environmental fluid dynamics, large-scale simulations and high-performance computing (HPC), data-driven and machine-learning approaches for flow problems, and shock-particle interaction and complex multiphysics applications. His group's work spans both industrial and environmental systems, from fuel sprays to volcanic plume dynamics, sediment transport, gravity currents, and airborne droplet transport relevant to indoor air quality.

bardosh_kevin.name: Kevin Bardosh
bardosh_kevin.role: Research Assistant Professor, Anthropology and Emerging Pathogens; Affiliate Faculty, UF Water Institute
education: Ph.D. International Development / African Studies, University of Edinburgh, UK
M.S. International Development, University of Edinburgh, UK
B.A. Philosophy & History of Science, University of British Columbia, Canada
bardosh_kevin.research_focus: Dr. Kevin Bardosh is an applied medical anthropologist and implementation scientist whose work bridges social science and global health. His research focuses on the biosocial dynamics of health and disease, infectious diseases and emerging pathogens, community-based and participatory approaches to public health interventions, implementation science and health systems, environmental anthropology and development studies, and zoonotic disease governance. His work integrates community engagement with public health strategy, including mosquito control initiatives for malaria, lymphatic filariasis, and arboviruses like Zika. He has conducted research in the Caribbean, Latin America, Africa, and Southeast Asia, collaborating with ministries, NGOs, and community partners on disease control and health policy implementation.

barnes_grenville.research_focus: Dr. Grenville Barnes is a globally recognized scholar in land tenure, cadastral systems, and geospatial governance, especially in the Global South. His work sits at the intersection of land administration and cadastral systems, GIS, property rights and land tenure security, participatory mapping, informal settlements and urbanization, land governance and policy, and geospatial approaches to social equity. He has led or contributed to land and geospatial projects across Africa (Ghana, Kenya, Uganda, Namibia, South Africa), the Caribbean, Latin America, and Southeast Asia. His work is frequently used by the World Bank, UN-Habitat, national land agencies, and NGOs working on land reform and urban informality.

barnett_cynthia.name: Cynthia Barnett
barnett_cynthia.role: Environmental Journalist, Author, and Professor; Affiliate Faculty, UF Water Institute
barnett_cynthia.research_focus: Cynthia Barnett is one of the most prominent environmental journalists in the United States, known especially for her work on water, climate, and environmental justice. She sits at the intersection of investigative journalism, environmental humanities, public-facing science communication, and water policy. Her narrative nonfiction has shaped public understanding of water scarcity, climate risk, and environmental justice. At UF, she teaches environmental journalism, mentors investigative reporters, and works closely with scientists to translate research into public impact. She served as faculty team member on UF Water Institute's Water Institute Graduate Fellows (WIGF) cohort alongside water-science faculty. She also serves as faculty advisor/editor for UF student investigative water reporting, including WUFT's statewide water-quality project "Watershed" funded by the Pulitzer Center.

barry_savanna.role: Extension Agent (Agriculture & Natural Resources); Affiliate Faculty, UF Water Institute
barry_savanna.research_focus: Savanna Barry is a UF/IFAS Extension Agent working at the interface of agriculture, natural resource management, applied environmental science, and community outreach. Her role focuses on translational science: taking UF research and converting it into actionable guidance for producers, land managers, and local stakeholders. She works at the implementation interface of Florida water, soil, and conservation policy, translating research and regulatory frameworks into locally adopted agricultural and land-stewardship practices. Her work supports compliance, adoption, and behavior change for water and conservation programs, which is where most environmental policy succeeds or fails.

bartels_wendylin.research_focus: Wendy-Lin Bartels is a human-dimensions and social science researcher whose work focuses on how people perceive, respond to, and govern environmental change. She operates at the intersection of climate adaptation, natural resource governance, social learning and decision-making, stakeholder engagement, and environmental communication. Her research centers on decision processes under uncertainty, especially in the context of climate and environmental change. She examines how managers, stakeholders, and communities interpret climate and hydrologic uncertainty, decision-making around water scarcity, flooding, and extreme events, and institutional and behavioral barriers to adaptive water management. Her work explains why technically sound environmental solutions succeed or fail once people and institutions are involved.

His work includes variable rate irrigation (VRI) technology, impacts of saltwater intrusion and flooding on agricultural soils, and irrigation systems for tropical and subtropical fruit crops.

bean_eban.research_focus: Eban Bean is an agricultural engineer specializing in water, nutrients, and agroecosystem sustainability, with a strong emphasis on applied, systems-level solutions. His research focuses on measuring, modeling, and reducing agricultural and urban impacts on water resources, especially in nutrient-sensitive systems. Key areas include nitrogen and phosphorus transport, agricultural runoff and leaching, edge-of-field and watershed-scale monitoring, Best Management Practices (BMPs) evaluation, modeling tools (SWAT, APEX, field-scale nutrient budgets), and climate variability and resilience in agrohydrologic systems. He produces the empirical evidence used to design and evaluate agricultural and urban BMPs and BMAP strategies for nutrient reduction and water-quality protection in Florida.

B.S. Environmental Horticulture, Cal Poly Pomona
beeson_richard.research_focus: Richard Beeson is a leading researcher in environmental horticulture and plant-water relations, with nationally recognized work on irrigation efficiency, water use, and sustainable landscape management. His research focuses on quantifying and improving water-use efficiency in managed plant systems, including plant water relations and evapotranspiration, irrigation scheduling and sensor-based control, nursery and landscape water demand, container-grown ornamental production, water conservation strategies for urban landscapes, and climate effects on plant water use. He provides the scientific basis for irrigation efficiency and landscape water-conservation practices that support Florida's water-supply sustainability and springs protection goals.

behringer_donald.research_focus: Dr. Behringer is a Professor of Marine and Disease Ecology. His research focuses on marine disease ecology and epidemiology, the resilience and restoration of marine communities impacted by human or natural disturbances, and the ecology and behavior of marine invertebrates. He discovered PaV1, a novel virus infecting Caribbean spiny lobsters - the only naturally occurring virus known to infect lobsters. His lab studies cyanobacteria blooms on sponge habitat loss in the Florida Keys and temperature effects on host-virus relationships.

education: Ph.D., Universita di Firenze-Universite de Nantes
belletti_gabriele.research_focus: Dr. Belletti's research encompasses environmental humanities, 20th and 21st-century poetics, migration studies, and literary connections between Italy and France. He is Principal Investigator of the SHINE Project (Sciences Humanities Intelligence Nurturing Emotions), an interdisciplinary initiative exploring how poetic texts and immersive virtual environments affect emotional engagement and ecological awareness. The project integrates AI, literary analysis, neuroscience, and environmental humanities to foster empathy and pro-environmental behaviors.

B.S. Agriculture, University of Florida
bhadha_jehangir.research_focus: Jehangir Bhadha is a soil and water quality scientist whose work is central to nutrient management, phosphorus dynamics, and agricultural sustainability in the Everglades Agricultural Area (EAA). His research is highly applied and policy-facing, focusing on phosphorus dynamics in organic and mineral soils, EAA soils and histosols, nutrient runoff and leaching, Best Management Practices (BMPs) evaluation, soil testing and nutrient recommendations, and water-quality monitoring and compliance science. His work directly informs regulatory thresholds, BMP manuals, and Everglades restoration strategies. As an Extension specialist at EREC, he works directly with producers to translate research into field-ready BMP guidance and supports regulatory compliance without productivity loss.

bisesi_joseph.name: Joseph Bisesi
bisesi_joseph.role: Associate Professor; Affiliate Faculty, UF Water Institute

B.S. Biology, University of South Carolina
bisesi_joseph.research_focus: Joseph Bisesi is an environmental health scientist and toxicologist whose work focuses on contaminants in water and the human health risks they pose, especially cyanotoxins from harmful algal blooms (HABs), drinking water contaminants, and emerging pollutants. His research is highly applied and policy-relevant, covering cyanotoxins (microcystins, cylindrospermopsin), HABs and human exposure pathways, drinking water contamination, risk assessment and regulatory thresholds, environmental exposure modeling, and public health surveillance and response. His research frequently informs health advisories, monitoring strategies, and regulatory discussions. He serves as a health-impact translator, connecting water contamination to measurable human risk and policy decisions.

bodrey_ray.research_focus: Ray Bodrey is County Extension Director and Extension Agent III for Gulf County in Northwest Florida, with a dual role as Marine Extension Agent III through the Florida Sea Grant College Program. His work translates UF research into on-the-ground practice, supporting producers, landowners, and coastal communities. His academic background and current Ph.D. work focus on soil and water sciences, with emphasis on soil health strategies especially after land conversion. He operates at the implementation interface of Florida water, soil, and conservation policy, supporting adoption of best management practices through UF/IFAS Extension.

bohlman_stephanie.name: Stephanie Bohlman

bohlman_stephanie.research_focus: Stephanie Bohlman is a forest ecologist and remote sensing scientist whose work focuses on how forests respond to climate variability, disturbance, and land-use change, particularly in tropical and subtropical systems. Her research emphasizes scaling forest processes from plots to regions, including forest structure and biomass, carbon stocks and fluxes, drought and climate stress, disturbance ecology (fire, storms, land use), and remote sensing of vegetation using LiDAR, Landsat, and MODIS. She studies tropical forests (Amazon, Central America) and subtropical systems, using field data combined with remote sensing to quantify ecosystem responses at scale. Her expertise in hurricane disturbance impacts, drought stress, and fire-climate interactions is directly relevant to Florida forest management and climate adaptation planning.

bolger_lexi.research_focus: Lexi Bolger is a Ph.D. student in Interdisciplinary Ecology pursuing a career in environmental education and communication. For her dissertation, Lexi plans to develop, implement, and assess a multicultural community bioblitz. Previously a Graduate Student Coordinator at the UF Thompson Earth Systems Institute, Lexi has experience in outreach, coordinating professional development, and science communication.

bonzongo_jeanclaude.role: Professor; Affiliate Faculty, UF Water Institute

bonzongo_jeanclaude.research_focus: Jean-Claude Bonzongo is a leading environmental chemist and biogeochemist internationally recognized for his work on trace metals, especially mercury (Hg), in aquatic and terrestrial systems. His research focuses on how contaminants behave once they enter natural systems, including mercury biogeochemistry (methylation, demethylation), sediment-water interactions, bioavailability and bioaccumulation, atmospheric deposition and watershed transport, aquatic food webs, and trace-metal contamination in tropical, temperate, and engineered systems. His work spans field studies, laboratory experiments, and analytical method development. He explains how toxic metals move, transform, and accumulate in real ecosystems, providing the mechanistic science behind mercury transformation, bioaccumulation, and exposure risk in water bodies.

Current investigations center on per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), lipidomics, and metabolomics applications across environmental and clinical domains.

braswell_anna.research_focus: Dr. Braswell is a coastal ecosystem ecologist interested in the formation, persistence, and degradation of coastal ecosystems within the context of surrounding terrestrial, marine, and urban systems. She uses geospatial data analysis and empirical field data to understand these complex socio-environmental systems. Her interdisciplinary research links ecological, geomorphic, and demographic data within a macroscale ecology framework. Through her cross-appointment with Florida Sea Grant, she works with stakeholders to create tools for coastal watershed and ecosystem management.

bravo_lorna.research_focus: Lorna Bravo is a horticultural scientist whose work focuses on sustainable crop production in subtropical and tropical environments, with particular attention to vegetable and specialty crops grown under South Florida conditions. Her research emphasizes vegetable and specialty crop management, nutrient management and fertilization strategies, crop response to environmental stress, sustainable production practices, and yield and quality optimization. She conducts grower-relevant field trials designed to translate quickly into production guidance. Her work supports improved fertilizer efficiency and reduced nutrient losses under South Florida's high rainfall and sandy/organic soil conditions.

M.S. Limnology, University of Minnesota
B.A. Biology / Environmental Sciences
brenner_mark.research_focus: Mark Brenner is an internationally recognized paleolimnologist and environmental geochemist whose work reconstructs long-term environmental, climate, and water-quality change using lake sediment records. His research integrates geology, chemistry, and ecology to reconstruct past environments, including paleolimnology from lake sediment cores, water-quality history (nutrients, eutrophication), climate variability and change, carbon and nutrient cycling, isotope geochemistry, and human impacts on aquatic systems. He works across temporal scales from decades to millennia, providing the historical baselines needed to understand whether modern environmental change is unprecedented, accelerated, or policy-driven. He has extensive experience in Florida, the Caribbean, and Central America.

brisotto_carla.research_focus: Carla Brisotto is an institute-level research administrator and program leader whose work focuses on building, coordinating, and scaling interdisciplinary resilience research at the university-government-industry interface. As Associate Director of FIBER, she plays a central role in translating research into deployable resilience solutions, coordinating across engineering, planning, policy, and environmental sciences, managing large-scale projects and stakeholder relationships, and supporting grants, partnerships, and strategic initiatives. Her functional expertise includes program and portfolio management, cross-disciplinary team building, government and community engagement, applied research deployment, and strategic planning for resilience initiatives.

broadbent_eben.research_focus: Eben Broadbent is a forest ecologist and remote-sensing scientist internationally recognized for his work on forest disturbance, land-use change, and ecosystem recovery, especially in tropical forests. His research integrates ecology, geospatial analysis, and climate science, covering tropical forest ecology, deforestation and forest degradation, land-use and land-cover change, forest carbon dynamics, disturbance and recovery trajectories, remote sensing (Landsat, MODIS, LiDAR), and coupled human-natural systems. He quantifies how forests respond to human disturbance and climate stress using satellite data, LiDAR, and long-term field studies, combining satellite time series with field plots for causal inference about disturbance and recovery.

His work centers on applied agriculture, natural resources, and community education, translating university research into practical guidance for producers, land managers, and local stakeholders. He operates at the ground level where policy gets executed, serving as a trusted intermediary between regulators and producers, between research and real-world constraints, and between conservation goals and economic realities.

canestrelli_alberto.research_focus: Dr. Canestrelli's primary research focus is on numerical modeling of coastal and estuarine hydrodynamics and morphodynamics. He studies the long-term morphodynamic evolution of river estuaries and back barrier basins, with particular focus on salt marsh erosion and progradation. His research also addresses storm surge forecasting, beach recovery modeling, nutrient dispersal in estuarine waters, and the impact of crab grazing on tidal creek hydrodynamics.

capasso_jay.research_focus: Jay Capasso is a specialized Extension agent focused on agricultural water resources and nutrient management in Northeast Florida. He combines research, on-farm trials, stakeholder education, and technology transfer to help growers and county faculty implement science-based water, nutrient, and irrigation best management practices. His work centers on irrigation efficiency and distribution uniformity, nutrient management and soil moisture monitoring including sensor networks, and multi-county water quality and nutrient cycling outreach. He contributes to applied research such as the Sod-Based Rotation Project at NFREC-Suwannee Valley, evaluating how sod-based crop rotations affect soil health, water holding capacity, nitrate leaching, and nutrient cycling. His Google Scholar profile shows 190+ citations.

carney_jeffrey.research_focus: Jeffrey Carney is a Professor of Architecture and founding Director of the Florida Institute for Built Environment Resilience (FIBER). His work focuses on climate adaptation and community resilience in the built environment, particularly in coastal and flood-prone regions. He leads interdisciplinary research and engagement programs that help Florida communities prepare for climate impacts through innovative design, planning, and technology. His research areas include climate adaptation strategies for buildings and communities, urban digital twins for resilience planning, delta and coastal urbanism, community-engaged resilience planning, and the intersection of design and climate policy. Through FIBER, he coordinates university-wide efforts to support resilient design and planning across Florida.

carr_margaret.name: Margaret Carr
carr_margaret.role: Professor; Affiliate Faculty, UF Water Institute
carr_margaret.academic_unit: Department of Landscape Architecture, College of Design, Construction and Planning
carr_margaret.research_focus: Margaret Carr is a landscape architect and urban systems thinker whose work focuses on how landscapes can be designed to support environmental performance, resilience, and livability, especially in urban and suburban contexts. Her work sits at the intersection of landscape design, urban ecology, and environmental planning, including urban stormwater and green infrastructure, landscape performance and resilience, climate-responsive urban design, public space and environmental function, human-environment interaction, and spatial planning for sustainability. Her work emphasizes how design decisions influence environmental outcomes, especially water movement, heat, and habitat. She bridges environmental goals, spatial design, and everyday use, ensuring that policy intent survives implementation at the design stage.

cartondegrammont_paloma.name: Paloma Carton de Grammont
cartondegrammont_paloma.academic_unit: UF Water Institute, University of Florida
cartondegrammont_paloma.research_focus: Dr. Carton de Grammont is an environmental geographer and trained facilitator who designs and guides processes that allow groups to advance their ideas and reach agreement by encouraging full and equitable participation. With over 15 years of experience, she specializes in coordinating interdisciplinary, multi-institutional and international teams that conduct research and develop policies in diverse topics of natural resource management and environmental change. Her career spans academic institutions, government agencies, and nonprofit organizations in both the United States and Mexico.

cash_elizabeth.name: Elizabeth Cash
cash_elizabeth.research_focus: Elizabeth Cash's research centers on the ecology and evolution of social insects, with particular focus on the diversity, function, and evolution of traits involved in communication and group living in native and introduced ant species. She uses an integrative approach combining chemical ecology, behavior, genomics, and functional genetics. Key research areas include pheromone communication, invasive ant biology, desaturase genes in social Hymenoptera, and social immunity through natural antibiotics produced by ants.

chagaris_david.name: David Chagaris
chagaris_david.role: Research Associate Professor; Affiliate Faculty, UF Water Institute
chagaris_david.research_focus: David Chagaris is an expert in ecosystem-based fisheries science who develops and applies quantitative food-web and ecosystem models to understand how marine fish populations and broader ecosystems respond to fishing pressure, environmental change, policy options and management strategies, and trophic interactions and predator-prey dynamics. He combines mechanistic models (e.g., Ecopath with Ecosim) with empirical data to inform science-based decision making in marine resource management. His research is especially relevant to Gulf of Mexico and West Florida Shelf marine ecosystems, which tie into coastal ecology, climate influences, and fisheries sustainability.

chandler_ron.research_focus: Ron Chandler is an Associate Instructional Professor whose work bridges psychology, sustainability, and environmental decision making. His unique background spans ecology, hydrology, and psychology, from early work in water quality monitoring and lake/stream ecology to leadership of conservation initiatives and eventual doctoral study in psychology. He develops and teaches Sustainability Psychology, an emerging field that uses psychological theory to improve the effectiveness of sustainability education and programs. His interests include human dignity, prosocial behavior, and environmental identity as drivers of sustainable action, as well as how cultural and social factors influence environmental decisions and behaviors. He founded the Conservation Initiative for the Asian Elephant, focusing on holistic, cross-cultural approaches to human-wildlife coexistence.

chen_jianjun.research_focus: Jianjun Chen is an internationally recognized environmental horticulturist and plant physiologist specializing in ornamental and foliage plant production in greenhouse, nursery, and interior landscapes. His research spans plant physiology, propagation, and production systems with strong applied focus, including ornamental and foliage plant production, plant nutrition and substrate management, plant growth regulation, physiological responses to environmental stress, and interior plantscape performance. He works to optimize plant growth, quality, and sustainability under controlled and semi-controlled environments, frequently integrating basic plant physiology with commercial production challenges.

christner_brent.research_focus: Brent Christner is an internationally recognized environmental microbiologist studying microbial life in extreme environments, particularly cold ecosystems such as glaciers, snow, ice cores, and the atmosphere. His work spans microbiology, Earth science, and climate processes, including microbial ecology of ice, snow, and glacial systems, atmospheric microbiology (microbes in clouds, precipitation, aerosols), ice-nucleating bacteria and proteins, biogeochemical cycling in extreme environments, and microbial survival and activity at low temperatures. His research integrates field sampling, molecular biology, and geochemical analysis to understand how microorganisms influence and are influenced by cryosphere processes, atmospheric chemistry, and biogeochemical cycles.

His expertise centers on below-ground processes explaining how roots, microbes, and soil chemistry control nutrient availability, carbon storage, and nutrient losses in managed and natural ecosystems. His research areas include soil nutrient cycling (N, P, Ca, Mg), soil organic matter and carbon dynamics, forest and agroforestry soils, root-soil-microbe interactions, nutrient limitation and fertilization effects, and tropical forest biogeochemistry.

She studies how institutions, incentives, and social dynamics shape environmental outcomes, especially in protected areas, community-based conservation, and climate adaptation contexts. Her research areas include human dimensions of conservation, community-based natural resource management, environmental governance and institutions, equity, justice, and participation in conservation, climate adaptation and resilience, and protected areas and land-use decision-making. Her work explains why conservation practices that affect soils, forests, and waters succeed or fail.

daugherty_jamielyn.role: Extension Agent; Affiliate Faculty, UF Water Institute
daugherty_jamielyn.research_focus: Jamielyn Daugherty is a UF/IFAS Extension Agent serving Lake County, working at the interface of agriculture, natural resources, and community education. Her role centers on delivering UF/IFAS science to producers and residents to improve land management, water quality, and environmental stewardship. Her program areas include agricultural and natural resource outreach, water quality and nutrient stewardship, soil and land management practices, producer and community education, and adoption of UF/IFAS BMPs. She supports both agricultural producers and non-agricultural land managers in this mixed rural-exurban county.

davis_jack.name: Jack Davis
davis_jack.role: Professor of History; Affiliate Faculty, UF Water Institute
davis_jack.research_focus: Jack Davis is one of the most influential environmental historians in the United States, best known for his work on American environmental history, conservation policy, and human-nature relationships, with a strong emphasis on Florida, the American South, and wetlands. His research areas include environmental history, conservation and land-use policy, wetlands, water, and coastal environments, race, power, and environmental governance, and long-run institutional drivers of ecological change. His work is foundational for understanding why today's water, soil, and nutrient problems exist.

dehoe_guilhem.research_focus: Dr. De Hoe's research centers on dynamic networks, polyesters, and biodegradation. His work investigates the intersection of polymer chemistry and environmental sustainability, with emphasis on developing materials that can degrade responsibly while maintaining functional polymeric properties.

Her research explores how anthropogenic activities — including contamination, waste streams, land-use change and disturbances — affect microbiomes, biogeochemical cycles, pathogen dynamics and exposure risk in water systems. She also engages in community-engaged research to reduce environmental health disparities and enhance resilience in vulnerable communities. dennis_nicole.research_focus: Nicole Dennis is an environmental health scientist and exposure toxicologist whose work focuses on cyanobacterial harmful algal blooms (cyanoHABs), cyanotoxins (e.g., microcystins), and human health risk from waterborne contaminants. Her research areas include cyanobacterial harmful algal blooms (HABs), cyanotoxins (microcystins, cylindrospermopsin, saxitoxins), human exposure pathways (drinking water, recreation, aerosols), environmental epidemiology, risk assessment and public health thresholds, and climate-water-health interactions. Her work directly connects nutrient enrichment of waters to public-health consequences.

denslow_nancy.research_focus: Nancy Denslow is an internationally recognized toxicologist and toxicogenomics pioneer, best known for developing molecular and genomic tools to detect endocrine-disrupting chemicals (EDCs) and other contaminants in aquatic organisms. Her research areas include endocrine disruption in aquatic organisms, toxicogenomics and transcriptomics, biomarkers of contaminant exposure, fish and wildlife health, estrogenic and anti-androgenic compounds, and risk assessment for environmental contaminants. Her work is foundational for modern molecular biomonitoring of water quality, identifying sub-lethal biological responses to pollutants long before population collapse or visible ecosystem damage occurs.

His research areas include marine and estuarine microbial ecology, bacterioplankton dynamics, carbon cycling and microbial respiration, nitrogen and phosphorus utilization, microbial food webs, and responses of microbes to nutrient enrichment and environmental change. His work explains how microbial community composition and metabolism regulate carbon and nutrient fluxes in aquatic ecosystems.

Her research areas include aquatic and environmental microbiology, microbial community ecology, anaerobic and low-oxygen systems, microbial metabolism under environmental stress, biogeochemical cycling of carbon and nutrients, and molecular and genomic tools in environmental systems. Her research areas include environmental governance and policy, political ecology, land-use change and sustainability transitions, human-environment interactions, power, equity, and environmental decision-making, and qualitative and mixed spatial methods. Her work explains why technically sound environmental solutions succeed or fail once they encounter real social and political landscapes.

estrada_james.role: Research Scientist / Agronomist; Affiliate Faculty, UF Water Institute
estrada_james.research_focus: James Estrada is an agronomist whose research focuses on soil fertility, nutrient management, and crop production systems in Florida agricultural settings. His work centers on optimizing nitrogen use efficiency and developing practical nutrient management strategies that maintain crop productivity while reducing environmental losses. His research areas include soil fertility and plant nutrition, nitrogen management and use efficiency, crop production system optimization, nutrient cycling in agricultural systems, and fertilizer timing and placement strategies. His work directly supports agricultural BMPs by identifying practices that reduce nutrient losses to groundwater and surface water while sustaining farm profitability.

evans_austin.name: Austin Evans
evans_austin.role: Assistant Professor; Affiliate Faculty, UF Water Institute
evans_austin.research_focus: Austin Evans is a chemist whose work sits at the intersection of chemical analysis, molecular processes, and environmental or materials-relevant chemistry. His research emphasizes chemical mechanisms and measurements that are foundational for understanding environmental transformations, reactivity, and contaminant behavior. His research areas include chemical reaction mechanisms, analytical and instrumental chemistry, molecular transformations relevant to environmental systems, chemical pathways affecting pollutant fate, and laboratory-based experimental chemistry. His work is typically upstream and enabling rather than policy-facing, but critical for validating assumptions used in environmental and nutrient models.

His work underpins industry standards and regulatory guidance for nutrient management in container production, designing systems that deliver the right water and nutrients at the right time to minimize leaching, runoff, and waste while maintaining crop quality.

fletcher_robert.research_focus: Robert Fletcher is an internationally recognized landscape ecologist and conservation scientist whose work focuses on how spatial patterns, habitat connectivity, and human land use affect biodiversity and ecosystem function. His research areas include landscape ecology, habitat fragmentation and connectivity, metapopulation and community dynamics, conservation planning and reserve design, human land use impacts, ecosystem services and biodiversity outcomes, and spatial modeling and synthesis. His work is foundational for translating land-use patterns into ecological consequences, connecting land management decisions to ecological resilience, species persistence, and ecosystem services across space and time.

fraisse_clyde.research_focus: Clyde Fraisse is a leading agricultural climatologist and climate-risk engineer specializing in climate variability, seasonal forecasting, decision support, and adaptation for agriculture. His work translates weather and climate signals into actionable management decisions for farmers, Extension, and policymakers. His research areas include agricultural climatology, seasonal climate forecasting, climate risk and decision support, crop-climate interactions, water management under climate variability, climate adaptation for agriculture, and Extension decision tools including AgroClimate. His work underpins climate-informed BMPs across Florida and the Southeast.

francisfloyd_ruth.role: Professor and Extension Veterinarian; Affiliate Faculty, UF Water Institute
francisfloyd_ruth.research_focus: Ruth Francis-Floyd is a nationally recognized aquatic animal health veterinarian whose work focuses on fish health, aquaculture systems, disease diagnostics, and water quality management. Her research areas include aquatic animal health and pathology, aquaculture systems management, fish disease diagnosis and prevention, water quality management for aquaculture, Extension education for producers and agencies, and risk communication for aquatic health. She explains how water chemistry, nutrients, and management practices directly affect fish health, disease risk, and system sustainability, translating veterinary and aquatic science into practical guidance for producers, Extension agents, regulators, and hobbyists.

frank_kathryn.role: Professor; Affiliate Faculty, UF Water Institute
frank_kathryn.academic_unit: Department of Design, Construction and Planning
frank_kathryn.research_focus: Kathryn Frank is a leading scholar in urban design, resilience, and landscape planning, with a strong focus on how the built environment shapes environmental performance, human behavior, and ecological outcomes. Her research areas include urban design and sustainability, climate adaptation and resilience, green infrastructure and nature-based solutions, stormwater and runoff-sensitive design, human behavior and environmental performance, urban heat, flooding, and climate risk, and planning and design evaluation. Her work bridges design theory, empirical analysis, and policy-relevant planning practice, studying how urban form, green infrastructure, and planning decisions influence stormwater management, nutrient runoff, climate resilience, and long-term sustainability.

frederick_peter.name: Peter Frederick
frederick_peter.role: Professor; Affiliate Faculty, UF Water Institute
frederick_peter.academic_unit: Department of Wildlife Ecology and Conservation
frederick_peter.research_focus: Peter Frederick is a wildlife ecologist specializing in wading birds as bioindicators of ecosystem health, particularly how nutrient loading, contaminants (especially mercury), and hydrologic alteration affect reproduction, behavior, and population viability in wetland systems. His research areas include wildlife ecology and conservation biology, wading bird ecology (ibis, herons, storks), ecotoxicology (mercury, trace metals), nutrient enrichment and food-web effects, and wetland and estuarine ecosystem responses to anthropogenic stressors. His long-term empirical research, especially in the Florida Everglades, translates nutrient and contaminant dynamics into biological consequences, validating whether nutrient management targets are ecologically meaningful.

fujimoto_masanori.academic_unit: Department of Soil and Water Sciences
fujimoto_masanori.research_focus: Masanori Fujimoto is a hydrologist and soil-water scientist specializing in field-scale and watershed-scale hydrology and water-quality research, with a focus on nutrient transport (nitrogen and phosphorus), hydrologic controls on nutrient loss, and monitoring/modeling of managed landscapes in Florida and comparable humid regions. His research areas include watershed hydrology, nutrient transport and fate (N, P), surface-groundwater interactions, water-quality monitoring and modeling, and effects of land use and management on runoff and leaching. His work directly informs best management practices (BMPs), TMDLs, and watershed restoration planning.

His research areas include agricultural and environmental communication, public understanding of science, risk communication and trust, science framing and messaging strategies, and stakeholder engagement and behavior change. His work targets the translation and adoption interface, shaping how scientific and technical knowledge becomes actionable for producers, communities, and policymakers.

Her research areas include landscape architecture and regional planning, environmental and climate-resilient design, spatial analysis and GIS-based decision support, nature-based solutions and green infrastructure, and visualization of environmental risk and systems. Her work translates complex environmental processes into decision-support landscapes and planning frameworks, operating at the interface between science and implementation.

garcia_alfredo.research_focus: Alfredo Garcia is an operations research scholar specializing in optimization and decision-making under uncertainty for complex, resource-constrained systems. His research areas include operations research and optimization, stochastic and robust decision-making, resource allocation under uncertainty, risk-aware systems design, and infrastructure and network optimization. His work focuses on how to allocate limited resources optimally when systems are dynamic, uncertain, and multi-objective, developing formal decision and optimization frameworks that are implemented by engineers, planners, and policymakers.

garrett_karen.role: Professor; Affiliate Faculty, UF Water Institute
garrett_karen.research_focus: Karen Garrett is a plant disease epidemiologist specializing in climate- and landscape-driven pathogen dynamics in agricultural systems. Her research areas include plant disease epidemiology, landscape and spatial ecology of pathogens, climate-disease interactions, risk modeling and forecasting, and agroecosystem resilience and management. Her work explicitly connects ecological theory, mathematical modeling, and applied plant health, examining how climate, landscape structure, and management practices shape the spread, persistence, and risk of plant pathogens.

gerber_stefan.name: Stefan Gerber
gerber_stefan.research_focus: Dr. Gerber's research aims to understand the cycles of carbon, nitrogen, and other nutrients in land ecosystems using modeling tools. His work addresses how humans interfere with natural biogeochemical cycles through pollution, fertilizers, and land-use, both directly and indirectly through anthropogenic climate change. A central question is how nutrient cycles and climate affect terrestrial biosphere capacity to sequester fossil CO2. Research includes effects of prescribed fire on soil microbial communities in Florida rangelands and nitrogen cycle representation in Earth system models.

gettys_lyn.research_focus: Lyn Gettys is an aquatic and wetland plant ecologist and extension specialist focused on integrated management of invasive aquatic weeds and restoration of native aquatic plants in Florida water systems. Her research areas include aquatic and wetland plant ecology, integrated aquatic weed management (chemical, biological, cultural), native aquatic plant culture and restoration, effects of nutrients and environmental conditions on aquatic vegetation, and Extension delivery of best management practices for aquatic systems. Her work blends fundamental plant ecology with applied control and restoration techniques relevant to aquatic ecosystems.

gillooly_james.role: Professor; Affiliate Faculty, UF Water Institute
gillooly_james.research_focus: James Gillooly is a theoretical ecologist known for metabolic scaling laws that link body size and temperature to biological rates across ecosystems. His research areas include metabolic theory of ecology, ecophysiology and bioenergetics, temperature dependence of biological processes, scaling laws across biological organization, and links between organismal metabolism and ecosystem processes. His work helped formalize the Metabolic Theory of Ecology (MTE), which links physiology to ecosystem-scale patterns and provides the theoretical basis for predicting how climate change alters biological activity.

Her research areas include soil-water processes, nutrient fate and transport (nitrogen, phosphorus), hydrologic and agro-environmental modeling, climate and management impacts on nutrient loss, and field-scale to watershed-scale analysis. Her work generates process-level and modeling evidence that feeds into BMP design, watershed assessment, and regulatory nutrient accounting.

He operates at the final mile of the research-to-implementation pipeline, where research and policy either succeed or fail based on uptake and behavior change.

grunwald_sabine.research_focus: Sabine Grunwald is a soil scientist and global leader in digital soil mapping and soil carbon modeling, specializing in spatial and temporal analysis of soil systems. Her research areas include digital soil mapping (DSM), soil carbon and soil health assessment, spatiotemporal soil modeling, soil degradation and land-use change, human-soil-landscape interactions, and machine learning and geospatial analytics in soil science. She is a foundational figure in data-driven soil science, generating methods, datasets, and conceptual frameworks that underpin soil monitoring programs, carbon accounting, and land-management decision systems.

His research areas include vegetable crop production and management, nutrient and fertility management in horticultural systems, cultivar evaluation and cropping systems, sustainable and resource-efficient horticulture, and grower-focused applied research. His work directly targets yield, quality, and environmental efficiency in intensive horticultural production, generating evidence that informs fertility recommendations, BMPs, and grower decision-making.

Current work includes simulation of water and nutrient transport for the upper Floridian aquifer and FACETS (Floridan Aquifer Collaborative Engagement for Sustainability).

hebert_david.research_focus: David Hebert is a county Extension agent focused on translating agricultural and natural-resource science into practical, locally adopted management practices. His program areas include Extension education and outreach, agriculture and natural-resource management, soil and water conservation practices, community engagement and local capacity building, and regulatory and BMP support at the county level. He operates at the local execution point where research findings and state-level policies are converted into real-world practices in Nassau County and Northeast Florida.

her_young.research_focus: Young Gu Her is an agro-hydrologic modeler specializing in watershed-scale simulation of nutrient and sediment transport under management and climate scenarios. His research areas include watershed hydrology and water-quality modeling, nutrient and sediment transport simulation, agroecosystem and landscape-scale modeling (e.g., SWAT and related frameworks), climate and land-use change scenario analysis, and decision-support systems for water management. His work emphasizes scenario analysis to evaluate how land management, climate variability, and policy choices affect water quality, developing models and decision-support tools that agencies, planners, and researchers use to test nutrient-reduction strategies.

kaminsky_laurel.research_focus: Laurel Kaminsky is an Environmental Sciences Librarian specializing in organizing, curating, and facilitating access to scientific information across disciplines such as soil, water, ecosystems, and natural resources. She has scholarly contributions in biodiversity data workflows and taxonomic collections digitization, including work on lichens and mosses ecology. Her role enables researchers, practitioners, and policy actors to discover, retrieve, and synthesize scientific literature and data essential for environmental research.

recent_publications.title: Think globally, barcode locally: nine years of macrofungi sampling reveals extensive biodiversity at the ordway-swisher biological station, a subtropical site in Florida
Assessment of Macrolichen Diversity at Ordway-Swisher Biological Station in Northern Florida Contributes to the Scientific Mission of NEON

kane_andrew.name: Andrew S. Kane, Ph.D.
kane_andrew.academic_unit: Environmental and Global Health, College of Public Health and Health Professions
education: Ph.D.
Associate Professor, Environmental and Global Health, University of Florida
Director, UF Aquatic Pathobiology Laboratory
kane_andrew.research_focus: Dr. Kane is an Associate Professor in Environmental and Global Health and Director of the UF Aquatic Pathobiology Laboratory. His research focuses on environmental toxicology, aquatic pathology, and environmental health at the interface of human populations with aquatic ecosystems. He investigates contaminant exposures, seafood safety, water quality stressors, behavioral toxicology, and environmental impacts on aquatic organisms. His work includes probabilistic risk assessment for seafood consumers, brevetoxin and saxitoxin impacts on fish behavior, and pollutant effects on aquatic species in Gulf of Mexico and coastal systems.

kaplan_david.name: David Kaplan
kaplan_david.role: Associate Professor; Director, H. T. Odum Center for Wetlands
kaplan_david.academic_unit: Department of Environmental Engineering Sciences, Engineering School of Sustainable Infrastructure & Environment (ESSIE), University of Florida
kaplan_david.research_focus: Dr. Kaplan’s research examines the **linkages among the hydrologic cycle, ecosystem processes and human activities**, with a focus on wetland, watershed and freshwater‐coastal interface systems. [oai_citation:1‡essie.ufl.edu](https://essie.ufl.edu/people/name/david-kaplan/?utm_source=chatgpt.com) He leads the Watershed Ecology Lab at UF, exploring how changes in land use, water withdrawals, restoration efforts, nutrient loads and climate variability influence hydrology, ecosystem function and management outcomes. [oai_citation:2‡watershedecology.org](https://www.watershedecology.org/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

A Sustainable Approach to Preserve the Choctawhatchee Coastal Dune Lakes of Florida

keellings_david.name: David Keellings
education: Associate Professor, Geography, University of Florida
keellings_david.research_focus: Dr. Keellings is an Associate Professor in Geography specializing in climate extremes, disaster climatology, and the interaction of weather with human systems. His research focuses on heat waves, tropical cyclones, rainfall extremes, climate risk, and social/environmental vulnerability across scales. He conducts climate data analysis and environmental hazard mapping for the southeastern United States, Caribbean, and tropical storm contexts, with applications to urban heat, rainfall intensity, and hazard exposure.

recent_publications.title: The Disproportionate Impact of COVID-19 among Undocumented Immigrants and Racial Minorities in the US
recent_publications.journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health

Application of Machine Learning Classifiers for Mode Choice Modeling for Movement-Challenged Persons

grants.funder: Directorate for Social, Behavioral & Economic Sciences

keiser_nick.name: Nick (Carl N.) Keiser
education: Assistant Professor, Biology, University of Florida
keiser_nick.research_focus: Dr. Keiser is an Assistant Professor in Biology specializing in behavioral ecology and infectious disease ecology. His research focuses on how animal behavior influences disease dynamics, especially in invertebrates such as spiders, flies, snails, ticks, and their associated parasites. His lab curates the Spider Parasite Digital Research (SPDR) Collection to study parasite-host interactions and parasite biodiversity. He investigates social determinants of infection spread and how individual and group behavior shapes transmission patterns.

kidambi_piran.name: Piran R. Kidambi, Ph.D.
education: Ph.D.
Associate Professor, Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, University of Florida
kidambi_piran.research_focus: Dr. Kidambi is an Associate Professor in Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering specializing in nanomaterials synthesis, process engineering, and in situ metrology. His research focuses on atomically thin membranes (graphene and 2D materials) for separations, energy applications, and advanced membrane technologies. He develops novel membrane materials and fabrication methods for molecular and ionic separations, with applications including desalination, dialysis, and nanofiltration.

kiker_gregory.name: Gregory Kiker
education: Ph.D. Agricultural and Biological Engineering, Cornell University (1998)
M.S. Agricultural and Biological Engineering, University of Florida (1992)
Fulbright Scholar for climate change research, South Africa (1992-1993)
kiker_gregory.research_focus: Dr. Kiker focuses on ecological modeling and management of natural systems. His work addresses the need for effective management of valued resources in uncertain environments through integration of ecological, hydrological, and decision modeling. Notable research includes the Florida-Georgia dispute over the Apalachicola-Chattahoochee-Flint Rivers, development of stakeholder-derived water system models, and coastal systems challenges.

recent_publications.title: A systematic review of economic assessments for brucellosis control interventions in livestock populations
Woody encroachment happens via intensification, not extensification, of species ranges in an African savanna
Household food waste behaviors of participants in a municipal community compost program
recent_publications.journal: Journal of Agriculture Food Systems and Community Development

recent_publications.title: Development of climate-smart sorghum ideotype for climate resilience in Ethiopia
Intra-group decision-making in agent-based models
