EAGER: IMPRESS-U Adaptive Infrastructure Recovery from Repeated Shocks through Resilience Stress Testing in Ukraine
EAGER International Type II: Inclusiveness and Diversity as Building Blocks of Resilient International Research Teams in the Age of COVID-19

kim_jinwon.name: Jin-Won Kim, Ph.D.
education: Ph.D.
Associate Professor, Tourism, Recreation & Sport Management, University of Florida
kim_jinwon.research_focus: Dr. Kim is an Associate Professor and interdisciplinary geographer whose work centers on how recreation, parks, tourism, and community dynamics contribute to sustainable, healthy, and resilient societies. His research blends spatial analysis, community development, policy, and sustainability using geospatial technologies including GIS, GPS, LiDAR, and remote sensing. He studies planning, development, and management of parks, recreation, and tourism resources in coastal and inland recreational landscapes.

kim_youngho.name: Youngho (Young) Kim, Ph.D.
kim_youngho.academic_unit: Food and Resource Economics (Environmental & Resource Economics)
education: Ph.D.
Assistant Professor, Food and Resource Economics, University of Florida (UF/IFAS)
kim_youngho.research_focus: Dr. Kim is an Assistant Professor in Food and Resource Economics specializing in the economic design and evaluation of environmental policy instruments. His research focuses on Payments for Ecosystem Services (PES) and environmental credit or trading markets that aim to correct market failures in environmental service provision, including flood mitigation, water quality improvement, and biodiversity outcomes. He develops quantitative economic models and policy evaluations used directly by environmental policy designers, conservation programs, and natural resource managers.

kjelgren_roger.name: Roger Kjelgren, Ph.D.
kjelgren_roger.academic_unit: UF/IFAS Mid-Florida Research and Education Center (MREC)
education: Ph.D.
Professor Emeritus / Former Director, Mid-Florida Research and Education Center, University of Florida
kjelgren_roger.research_focus: Dr. Kjelgren is a Professor Emeritus specializing in water use, water conservation, and plant-landscape relationships. His research applies horticultural and environmental science to quantify plant water use, irrigation efficiency, and sustainable landscape practices for urban and agricultural systems. He studies plant water stress responses, soil moisture dynamics, and water-wise landscape design, particularly for Florida and semi-tropical environments.

klizentyte_kotryna.name: Kotryna Klizentyte, Ph.D.
klizentyte_kotryna.academic_unit: School of Forest, Fisheries, and Geomatics Sciences
education: Ph.D.
Assistant Professor of Natural Resource Economics and Policy, University of Florida
klizentyte_kotryna.research_focus: Dr. Klizentyte is an Assistant Professor specializing in economic valuation of ecosystem services and natural resource policy analysis. She uses stated and revealed preference models to assess public preferences and economic tradeoffs connected to environmental management decisions such as conservation programs, recreation, and land use change. Her research generates quantitative evidence on values and preferences that inform policy design and evaluation for natural resource management, especially where economic incentives and ecosystem services intersect.

kohen_martha.name: Martha Kohen, Ph.D.
kohen_martha.academic_unit: School of Architecture – Design, Construction & Planning (DCP)
education: Ph.D.
Associate Professor, School of Architecture, University of Florida
kohen_martha.research_focus: Dr. Kohen is an Associate Professor in the School of Architecture specializing in architecture, urban design, community design processes, and participatory/equitable development. Her work emphasizes how design methods engage communities and shape public space, affordable housing, and spatial justice. She focuses on design as a civic, policy, and community practice, exploring how inclusive design and planning frameworks can lead to land-use outcomes that balance community needs with environmental resilience.

krimsky_lisa.name: Lisa Krimsky
krimsky_lisa.role: Regional Water Resources Extension Agent IV (Southeast District); Regional Specialized Agent, UF/IFAS Extension & Florida Sea Grant
krimsky_lisa.academic_unit: UF/IFAS Extension – Southeast District; Florida Sea Grant Program
krimsky_lisa.office: Indian River Research & Education Center, 2199 S Rock Rd, Ft Pierce, FL 34945
krimsky_lisa.research_focus: Dr. Lisa Krimsky’s extension and research work addresses coastal, urban and watershed water-resource challenges in Florida’s Southeast District, covering 12 counties from Brevard through Monroe. [oai_citation:1‡Florida Sea Grant](https://www.flseagrant.org/fsg-directory/dr-lisa-krimsky/?utm_source=chatgpt.com) Her programs focus on water-quality protection, harmful algal bloom (HAB) outreach and response, nutrient-pollution mitigation, septic-to-sewer conversion, landscape hydraulics, and water-resources literacy. She uses empirical surveys of homeowners and stakeholders, extension-education frameworks, and field-based monitoring to support public-policy and management responses. [oai_citation:2‡Ask IFAS - Powered by EDIS](https://edis.ifas.ufl.edu/experts/lkrimsky?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

recent_publications.title: Using a diffusion of innovation lens to understand homeowner support for septic system to sewer system conversions
How COVID-19 Exposed Water Supply Fragility in Florida, USA
Flushing Out Homeowner Perceptions to Improve Voluntary Septic to Sewer Conversion With Social Marketing

krueger_shelly.name: Shelly L. Krueger
krueger_shelly.academic_unit: Florida Sea Grant (UF/IFAS Extension, Monroe County)
krueger_shelly.research_focus: Shelly Krueger is a Sea Grant Extension Agent specializing in marine science-based Extension education and community outreach in the Florida Keys. She focuses on coastal water quality, habitat restoration, fisheries stewardship, and marine citizen science. As a marine biologist, she works directly with residents, volunteers, and agencies on practical environmental management and stewardship programs, converting coastal and marine science into community action.

lakesh_sharma.name: Lakesh K. Sharma, Ph.D.
lakesh_sharma.academic_unit: Soil and Water Sciences (Soil Fertility and Sustainable Agriculture Program)
education: Ph.D.
Assistant Professor, Soil and Water Sciences, University of Florida
lakesh_sharma.research_focus: Dr. Sharma is an Assistant Professor in Soil and Water Sciences specializing in soil fertility, nutrient management, and precision agriculture research. His work focuses on improving nutrient use efficiency and reducing nutrient loss through best management practices in cropping systems. He integrates agronomic, sensor, and economic methods to optimize fertilizer recommendations and minimize environmental impacts, particularly in Florida's sandy soils for crops including corn, cotton, potatoes, and hemp.

lamino_pablo.name: Pablo Lamino, Ph.D.
education: Ph.D.
Assistant Professor, Agricultural Education and Communication, University of Florida
lamino_pablo.research_focus: Dr. Lamino is an Assistant Professor specializing in agricultural leadership and communication research. His work focuses on leadership development, indigenous and rural communities, migration, environmental and agricultural perceptions, and intercultural competencies in both domestic and international agricultural contexts. He combines qualitative and mixed-methods approaches to understand community perspectives, behavior, and extension dynamics that influence agricultural and environmental practice adoption.

lang_timothy.name: Timothy A. Lang, Ph.D.
education: Ph.D.
Research Associate, Agricultural and Biological Engineering, University of Florida
lang_timothy.research_focus: Dr. Lang is a Research Associate at the Everglades Research and Education Center specializing in water quality and soil fertility research in the Everglades Agricultural Area (EAA). His work focuses on phosphorus dynamics, sediment-associated nutrient transport, and best management practices (BMPs) designed to reduce nutrient export from agricultural lands to downstream ecosystems. He quantifies biogeophysical controls on phosphorus transport including sediment adsorption/desorption, vegetation interactions, and hydrologic dynamics.

langeland_kenneth.name: Kenneth A. Langeland, Ph.D.
langeland_kenneth.academic_unit: Agronomy; Center for Aquatic and Invasive Plants

keywords: Center for Aquatic and Invasive Plants

education: Ph.D.
Professor Emeritus, Agronomy, University of Florida
langeland_kenneth.research_focus: Dr. Langeland is a Professor Emeritus specializing in aquatic and invasive plant management. His extensive career has focused on weed ecology, invasive species control, and integrated plant management, especially in Florida's natural and managed ecosystems. He produces actionable guidance through EDIS fact sheets, management plans, and technical reports on invasive plant management and integrated plant control that inform land managers, conservation practitioners, and policy.

larkin_iskande.name: Iskande V. Larkin, Ph.D.
larkin_iskande.research_focus: Dr. Larkin is a Senior Lecturer and Director of the Aquatic Animal Health Program at UF, specializing in aquatic animal health, marine mammal biology, and veterinary education. Her research focuses on Florida manatee physiology, reproduction, behavior, and conservation. She leads aquatic animal health education programs and develops online and in-person training in aquatic veterinary medicine, including application of machine learning to tissue health evaluation.

laughinghouse_dail.name: H. Dail Laughinghouse IV
laughinghouse_dail.academic_unit: Department of Agronomy (Ft. Lauderdale Research & Education Center), UF/IFAS
laughinghouse_dail.research_focus: Dr. Laughinghouse’s research centres on **phycology** (the study of algae) across a wide range of systems—from tropical to polar, freshwater to marine, applied to both ecosystem health and remediation. [oai_citation:2‡agronomy.ifas.ufl.edu](https://agronomy.ifas.ufl.edu/people/dail-laughinghouse-iv/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

laughinghouse_haywood.name: Haywood Dail Laughinghouse IV, Ph.D.
laughinghouse_haywood.academic_unit: Agronomy; Fort Lauderdale Research and Education Center (UF/IFAS)
education: Ph.D.
Associate Professor of Applied Phycology, University of Florida
laughinghouse_haywood.research_focus: Dr. Laughinghouse is an Associate Professor of Applied Phycology whose research spans cyanobacterial diversity, harmful algal blooms (HABs), toxin ecology, phycoremediation (algae-based remediation), bioremediation, and algal control in aquatic and terrestrial environments. His expertise blends ecology, systematics, toxicology, and applied mitigation strategies. He studies cyanobacterial taxonomy, genomics, molecular ecology, and algae-environment interactions in freshwater, marine, and terrestrial systems.

leibold_mathew.name: Mathew A. Leibold, Ph.D.
education: Ph.D.
Distinguished Professor, Biology, University of Florida
leibold_mathew.research_focus: Dr. Leibold is a Distinguished Professor known for his fundamental contributions to community and ecosystem ecology, particularly in metacommunity theory, biodiversity-ecosystem function relationships, ecological stoichiometry (nutrient ratios), food web dynamics, and spatial scaling of ecological processes. His work links species interactions to ecosystem processes such as nutrient cycling and primary productivity in aquatic and terrestrial systems. He studies how nutrient ratios (N:P:C) influence consumer-resource interactions, community structure, and ecosystem function.

leppla_norman.name: Norman C. Leppla, Ph.D.
education: Ph.D.
Professor Emeritus, Entomology and Nematology, University of Florida
leppla_norman.research_focus: Dr. Leppla is a Professor Emeritus known for applied insect ecology and pest management, especially in agricultural systems. His expertise includes integrated pest management (IPM), biological control, insect behavior and ecology, and decision support tools for managing insect pests in crops such as citrus, vegetables, and ornamentals. His work spans ecology, extension, and practical management of insect populations affecting crop production, with extensive contributions to natural enemy communities (parasitoids, predators) and host-plant-insect interactions.

lescord_gretchen.name: Gretchen Lescord, Ph.D.
lescord_gretchen.academic_unit: School of Forest, Fisheries, and Geomatics Sciences
education: Ph.D.
Assistant Professor of Applied Limnology, University of Florida
Director, Florida LAKEWATCH
lescord_gretchen.research_focus: Dr. Lescord is an Assistant Professor of Applied Limnology and Director of Florida LAKEWATCH, one of the largest long-running participatory water quality monitoring programs globally. Her research focuses on limnology and aquatic ecology, particularly water quality, contaminant ecology (mercury and arsenic), and food-web dynamics in lakes and rivers. She studies how contaminants bioaccumulate through trophic levels, how nutrient-linked trace metal dynamics interact with organic matter and aqueous chemistry, and how ecological indicators reflect lake condition.

leslie_lisa.name: Lisa Leslie, M.Ed.
leslie_lisa.academic_unit: Family, Youth and Community Sciences; UF/IFAS Extension Hillsborough County
education: M.Ed.
Program County Extension Agent IV, University of Florida
leslie_lisa.research_focus: Lisa Leslie is a Family and Consumer Sciences Extension Agent focused on personal finance education, financial literacy coaching, and community outreach programs. She develops and delivers educational content and services aimed at improving financial decision-making and household economic stability. Her work includes workshops, webinars, mentoring, and educational resources on budgeting, debt management, savings, credit scores, tax deductions, and estate planning for residents and families in Hillsborough County and broader Florida.

lester_william.name: William J. Lester, Ph.D.
education: Ph.D.
Extension Agent III Emeritus, University of Florida
lester_william.research_focus: William Lester is an Extension horticulture specialist with expertise in residential and commercial horticulture, plant medicine, and landscape/urban horticulture management. He translates horticultural science for homeowners, professionals, and Extension audiences and collaborates on natural resource and landscape topics including water quality-relevant practices. His work addresses landscape design, plant selection, and management practices that affect nutrient retention, runoff, and soil health in urban and suburban environments.

lewis_claire.name: Claire Lewis
lewis_claire.academic_unit: UF/IFAS Center for Land Use Efficiency (formerly Center for Landscape Conservation and Ecology)
lewis_claire.research_focus: Claire Lewis leads the Florida-Friendly Landscaping (FFL) Program, a statewide UF/IFAS Extension initiative that promotes sustainable, low-impact landscape practices to conserve water, protect environmental quality, and reduce pollutant loading into Florida's waters. Her work focuses on reducing water use and nutrient runoff from residential and urban landscapes by promoting appropriate plant selection, soil care, irrigation scheduling, and BMP adoption. She engages with homeowners, landscape professionals, builders, developers, HOAs, and community planning groups across Florida.

recent_publications.title: What is Florida-Friendly Landscaping™? EP607/ENH1343, 7/2021

li_jiayang.name: Jiayang Li, Ph.D.
li_jiayang.academic_unit: Landscape Architecture, College of Design, Construction and Planning
education: Ph.D.
Assistant Professor, Landscape Architecture, University of Florida
li_jiayang.research_focus: Dr. Li is an interdisciplinary landscape architect and scholar whose research focuses on ecological landscape design, climate resilience and adaptation, nature-based solutions (NBS), and human perceptions of landscape and ecological interventions. She integrates design thinking, social science methods, and community engagement to inform resilient landscape planning in the face of climate challenges. Her work addresses how nature-based solutions such as green infrastructure and floodable landscapes mediate hydrology, enhance infiltration, and improve pollutant retention including nutrient capture as part of resilient urban systems.

li_xiaoying.name: Xiaoying Li, Ph.D.
li_xiaoying.academic_unit: Horticultural Sciences; UF/IFAS Tropical Research and Education Center (TREC)
education: Ph.D.
Assistant Professor, Horticultural Sciences, University of Florida
li_xiaoying.research_focus: Dr. Li is known for vegetable crop science, sustainable production systems, disease and pathogen management, and crop diversification - especially in subtropical and tropical vegetable production contexts. Her research integrates crop physiology, plant pathology, and sustainable horticultural systems to improve the profitability and resilience of specialty and ethnic vegetable production in Florida. She works on crop introduction and diversification (edamame, okra), integrated pest and pathogen management (nematodes, soilborne diseases), and technology for disease detection and crop health monitoring.

li_yue.name: Yue Li, Ph.D.
li_yue.academic_unit: School of Forest, Fisheries, and Geomatics Sciences
education: Ph.D.
Assistant Professor of Environmental Education, University of Florida
li_yue.research_focus: Dr. Li is known for her work in environmental education theory and practice, focusing on how educators learn, change behavior, and engage communities around environmental and sustainability issues, including the innovative use of technology (such as AI) to enhance environmental learning and professional development for environmental educators. Her research examines sense of place, transformative learning, behavior change, and professional learning networks for environmental educators in formal and informal settings.

li_yuncong.name: Yuncong C. Li, Ph.D.
li_yuncong.academic_unit: Soil, Water and Ecosystem Sciences; UF/IFAS Tropical Research and Education Center (Homestead)
education: Ph.D.
Professor of Soil and Water Quality, University of Florida
li_yuncong.research_focus: Dr. Li is a soil and water quality scientist whose work focuses on nutrient cycling, fertilizer technology, soil chemistry, and water quality within agricultural and natural ecosystems - especially in the environmentally sensitive landscapes of South Florida such as the Everglades watershed and calcareous soils. His research addresses phosphorus and nitrogen dynamics in soils and sediments, controlled-release fertilizer development, soil testing calibration for calcareous soils, and water resource impacts including salinity intrusion on agricultural soils.

lin_yang.name: Yang Lin, Ph.D.
education: Ph.D.
Assistant Professor (Soil Health), University of Florida
lin_yang.research_focus: Dr. Lin is known for soil health science and biogeochemistry, focusing on soil organic matter dynamics, nutrient cycling (especially carbon, nitrogen, and phosphorus), soil redox processes, and implications for water quality and ecosystem function. Her work spans fundamental understanding of soil processes to applied nutrient management challenges. She studies soil organic matter persistence, microbial and chemical processes controlling nutrient release and retention, legacy nutrient behavior (biosolids, phosphorus), and landscape-scale nutrient transport in humid tropical and subtropical soils.

lin_zhoumeng.name: Zhoumeng Lin, B.Med, Ph.D., DABT, CPH, ERT, ATS
lin_zhoumeng.academic_unit: Environmental and Global Health, College of Public Health and Health Professions
education: B.Med; Ph.D.
DABT (Diplomate, American Board of Toxicology)
Associate Professor, Environmental and Global Health, University of Florida
lin_zhoumeng.research_focus: Dr. Lin is known for advancing computational toxicology and risk assessment, particularly through physiologically based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) modeling and machine learning/artificial intelligence approaches that characterize how chemicals, nanoparticles, drugs, and environmental contaminants behave in biological systems. His work spans toxicology, chemical risk assessment, nanomedicine, and food safety. He develops computational models for humans and multiple animal species to assess chemical and nanoparticle exposure pathways relevant to environment and food systems.

lindner_angela.name: Angela S. Lindner, Ph.D.
education: Ph.D., Civil and Environmental Engineering, University of Michigan
Associate Professor, Environmental Engineering Sciences, University of Florida
Interim Vice Provost for Undergraduate Affairs, University of Florida
lindner_angela.research_focus: Dr. Lindner is an environmental engineer whose scholarly focus has encompassed bioremediation, microbial oxidation of contaminants, sustainable engineering, pollution prevention, life cycle analysis, and green engineering principles. Her work spans environmental engineering fundamentals and applied environmental science, addressing environmental contaminant fate and transport, microbial transformation processes, and sustainability of engineered systems relevant to soils, water, and waste streams. She has taught courses in groundwater restoration, pollution prevention, green engineering, and environmental chemistry.

lindsey_alex.name: Alex J. Lindsey, Ph.D.
education: Ph.D.
Assistant Professor (Urban Turfgrass Management), University of Florida
lindsey_alex.research_focus: Dr. Lindsey is known for his research and extension in sustainable turfgrass and landscape management, with emphasis on optimizing turf performance, soil fertility and health, nutrient use efficiency, and reducing environmental impacts of management practices. He conducts field-focused experiments on turf management and sustainable nutrient/supplemental inputs including humic and algae-based fertilizers. His work addresses turfgrass species such as Kentucky bluegrass, bermudagrass, and zoysiagrass in Florida soils and climate contexts.

liu_mingjie.name: Mingjie Liu, Ph.D.
education: Ph.D.
Assistant Professor, Chemistry, University of Florida
liu_mingjie.research_focus: Dr. Liu is known for her computational and data-driven materials and molecular design research, focusing on machine learning and quantum chemistry methods to predict structure-property relationships of polymers, conjugated systems, and advanced materials. Her work blends density functional theory (DFT), machine learning, and materials informatics to accelerate discovery of functional molecular and polymeric materials. She develops high-throughput computational workflows and AI models linked with physical chemistry frameworks for materials prediction.

loiselle_bette.name: Bette A. Loiselle, Ph.D.
loiselle_bette.academic_unit: Wildlife Ecology and Conservation; Tropical Conservation and Development Program, Center for Latin American Studies
education: Ph.D.
Professor, Wildlife Ecology and Conservation, University of Florida
loiselle_bette.research_focus: Dr. Loiselle is a prominent neotropical ornithologist, ecologist, and conservation biologist known for her long-term, field-based research on tropical biodiversity, species interactions, seed dispersal, behavior and population ecology of birds, and species distribution modeling in tropical systems. Her work integrates spatial ecology, GIS, and long-term ecological data to understand ecological dynamics in neotropical landscapes of Ecuador, Costa Rica, Brazil, and other regions. She studies bird communities and their ecological roles in seed dispersal and forest processes.

loizzo_jamie.name: Jamie Loizzo
education: Ph.D. Learning Design & Technology, Purdue University
M.S.Ed. Learning Design & Technology, Purdue University
B.A. Radio Television (News), Southern Illinois University-Carbondale
loizzo_jamie.research_focus: Dr. Loizzo's research focuses on science communication and literacy, multimedia and mobile learning technologies, non-formal/informal science engagement, and arts-based research methodologies. She founded Streaming Science, a project-based learning and mobile electronic field trip program for 21st Century science communication education. She also developed the MOOCocracy platform for global science engagement and directs the SciLit Digital Research Group.

Conservation conversation: an arts-based approach to examine impacts of a live video webcast on youth viewers’ conceptualizations of forest ecosystems

Streaming Science: Electronic Field Trips
grants.funder: National Institute of Food and Agriculture

lorenzen_kai.name: Kai Lorenzen, Ph.D.
lorenzen_kai.academic_unit: Fisheries and Aquatic Sciences, School of Forest, Fisheries, and Geomatics Sciences
education: Ph.D.
Distinguished Professor of Integrative Fisheries Science, University of Florida
lorenzen_kai.research_focus: Dr. Lorenzen is known for integrative, trans-disciplinary research in fisheries science, combining quantitative ecology, population biology, human dimensions, and management science to address key global challenges in sustainable fisheries, fish stock assessment, enhancement and restoration, aquaculture systems, and resource conservation. His work bridges ecology with practical fisheries management worldwide, addressing marine, coastal, and inland fisheries systems globally as well as socio-ecological systems involving fisheries users and management institutions.

lorenzotrueba_jorge.name: Jorge Lorenzo-Trueba, Ph.D.
education: Ph.D.
Associate Professor, Geological Sciences, University of Florida
lorenzotrueba_jorge.research_focus: Dr. Lorenzo-Trueba is known for quantitative coastal geomorphology and landscape evolution science, using mathematical, numerical, and physical modeling to understand how coastal systems (barrier islands, deltas, marshes, shorelines) evolve under drivers like sea-level rise, sediment transport, storms, and human interventions. His work integrates models with field and experimental data to uncover controls on coastline change across decades to millennia. He leads the UF CoDyMo (Coastal Dynamics and Earth Systems Modeling) Laboratory, focusing on coupled natural-human coastal landscape dynamics.

ludgate_nargiza.name: Nargiza Ludgate, Ph.D.
ludgate_nargiza.academic_unit: Agricultural and Biological Engineering; UF/IFAS Everglades Research and Education Center
education: Ph.D.
Research Assistant Scientist, Agricultural and Biological Engineering, University of Florida
ludgate_nargiza.research_focus: Dr. Ludgate conducts applied research on food systems, agricultural development, and capacity building, often with an emphasis on gender, empowerment, and intersectionality in agricultural and nutrition pathways. Her work bridges engineering, food systems, and international development contexts, examining how socio-cultural factors shape agricultural practice adoption and outcomes. She focuses on smallholder and developing-country agricultural systems, gender and socio-cultural dimensions of farming and nutrition, and participatory development approaches.

lundy_lisa.name: Lisa K. Lundy, Ph.D.
education: Ph.D.
Professor, Agricultural Education and Communication, University of Florida
lundy_lisa.research_focus: Dr. Lundy is a scholar of agricultural and natural resource communication, especially public relations, science communication, visual communication (photography/media), and outreach strategy for agricultural and Extension audiences. Her work helps Extension professionals, scientists, and organizations communicate effectively with diverse publics and stakeholders. She studies strategic planning for Extension marketing, audience engagement, and crisis and risk communication in rural/agricultural contexts.

lusk_mary.name: Mary G. Lusk, Ph.D.
lusk_mary.academic_unit: Soil, Water, and Ecosystem Sciences; UF/IFAS Gulf Coast Research and Education Center
education: Ph.D.
Assistant Professor, Soil, Water, and Ecosystem Sciences, University of Florida
lusk_mary.research_focus: Dr. Lusk is known for research on urban soil and water quality, particularly how landscape management practices influence the storage, transport, and fate of nutrients and other constituents in managed urban environments. Her work integrates urban biogeochemistry, nutrient transport, stormwater runoff, and best management practice development. She studies nitrogen transformation, export, and retention in urban soils and stormwater pathways, septic system impacts on water quality, and spatial variability in soil nitrogen processes across mixed urban vegetation landscapes.

mackie_mickey.name: Mickey MacKie
education: Ph.D. Geophysics, Stanford University
mackie_mickey.research_focus: Dr. MacKie and the Gator Glaciology Lab investigate conditions beneath ice sheets and their influence on ice sheet evolution. Her research uses machine learning and geostatistical simulation methods to study the topographic and geologic characteristics of ice sheets. Field work is conducted in Svalbard and Greenland. The lab uses AI to analyze what lies beneath glaciers and how they are moving and melting, with implications for sea level rise projections.

mackowiak_cheryl.name: Cheryl L. Mackowiak, Ph.D.
mackowiak_cheryl.academic_unit: Soil, Water, and Ecosystem Sciences; UF/IFAS North Florida Research and Education Center
education: Ph.D.
Associate Professor, Soil, Water, and Ecosystem Sciences, University of Florida
mackowiak_cheryl.research_focus: Dr. Mackowiak is a soil nutrient management and water quality scientist whose research spans nutrient cycling, soil fertility, soil quality, and best management practices (BMPs) in agricultural and pastoral systems, with a strong emphasis on reducing nutrient losses and protecting water resources. She studies nitrogen leaching, phosphorus mobility, microbial-mediated nitrogen cycling, and soil landscape analysis in pasture, forage, and crop rotation systems in North Florida and Southeastern U.S.

marc_sarah.name: Sarah Marc
education: B.S. Environmental Science, University of Florida
marc_sarah.research_focus: Sarah graduated from the University of Florida with a Bachelor of Science in Environmental Science. She previously worked with UF/IFAS Extension to create educational materials focusing on Florida Friendly Landscaping, private wells and septic systems, and agricultural best management practices. As the Communications Specialist for the Water Institute, Sarah develops and implements communication strategies to support the Water Institute.

marcosmarin_francisco.name: Francisco Marcos Marin, Ph.D.
education: Ph.D.
Professor, Spanish and Portuguese Studies, University of Florida
marcosmarin_francisco.research_focus: Dr. Marcos Marin is an internationally recognized Hispanic linguist, philologist, and medievalist. His work focuses on the history of the Spanish language, Old Spanish and Old Portuguese texts, Romance philology, historical lexicography, and medieval Iberian literature and culture. He produces foundational scholarly resources including critical editions, historical linguistic analysis, and lexicographical contributions that support teaching, research, and cultural heritage preservation.

martenshabbena_willm.name: Willm Martens-Habbena
martenshabbena_willm.academic_unit: Fort Lauderdale Research and Education Center
education: Ph.D. Microbiology, University of Oldenburg, Germany (2006)
Diplom (Master-equivalent) Biology, University of Oldenburg (2001)
martenshabbena_willm.research_focus: Dr. Martens-Habbena's work sits at the intersection of microbiology, molecular biology and biogeochemistry. His research focuses on understanding how microorganisms drive biogeochemical cycles and their applications for human and environmental benefit. He investigates global change and sea level rise impacts on South Florida communities, explores microbial solutions to environmental challenges, and examines ecological interactions between microorganisms in complex ecosystems.

education: Ph.D.
Research Faculty, Nature Coast Biological Station, University of Florida
martin_charles.research_focus: Dr. Martin is a coastal and estuarine ecologist known for field-based and applied ecological research on estuaries, coastal food webs, introduced species, salinity and hydrology alteration, and habitat restoration and conservation in Gulf of Mexico coastal systems. His work addresses anthropogenic stressor effects on coastal systems, plant-animal and food web interactions, freshwater-salinity gradients, and marsh habitats in the Big Bend/Cedar Key region of Florida.

martin_ellen.name: Ellen Eckels Martin, Ph.D.
education: Ph.D.
Professor and Graduate Coordinator, Geological Sciences, University of Florida
martin_ellen.research_focus: Dr. Martin is a paleoceanographer and paleoclimatologist who uses radiogenic isotope geochemistry (Sr, Nd, Pb) preserved in marine sediments to reconstruct past ocean circulation, weathering processes, and climate change over geological time scales. Her research connects ocean circulation patterns, continental weathering, and biogeochemical cycles from the Cenozoic through glacial-interglacial periods. She studies deep ocean sediments, proglacial and deglaciated watershed environments, and global ocean basins including the Southern Ocean and Indian Ocean.

martin_jonathan.name: Jonathan B. Martin, Ph.D.
education: Ph.D.
Distinguished Professor and Associate Chair, Geological Sciences, University of Florida
martin_jonathan.research_focus: Dr. Martin is a hydrogeologist and geochemist known for his work on water-rock interactions, karst and coastal aquifers, groundwater-surface water exchange, and biogeochemical fluxes of solutes (including nutrients and carbon) through complex hydrogeologic systems, especially in Florida's carbonate aquifers and coastal settings. He studies the Floridan carbonate aquifer system and springs networks, coastal aquifers and subterranean estuaries (Indian River Lagoon, Yucatan, Bahamas), and deglaciated watersheds (Greenland) focusing on weathering and solute delivery.

martyniuk_christopher.name: Christopher Martyniuk

Her work spans approximately 80 publications on rainfall structure, spatial modeling, and extreme precipitation forecasting. Research systems include North Atlantic and Western Pacific tropical cyclones, coastal and inland U.S. regions affected by extreme rainfall, and weather impacts on communities including international case studies in Mozambique.

His work spans fundamental environmental engineering research and applied technologies that have been used or commercialized for water and air purification, influencing both engineering practice and environmental technology development. Research systems include engineered water treatment systems, air quality and gas-phase contaminant control, industrial and municipal pollution control technologies, and innovative materials for environmental remediation.

His work spans terrestrial ecosystems in Florida and global contexts (e.g., savannas in Africa), examining small mammals, mesopredators, and other vertebrate taxa, as well as wildlife responses to agricultural mosaics, urbanization, and conservation zones. His portfolio includes over 200 publications on wildlife ecology and conservation.

He uses bryophyte (moss) model systems — especially Ceratodon purpureus and Physcomitrella patens — to investigate processes like sex chromosome evolution, dioecy, and community genomics of moss-associated nitrogen fixation. His research spans Arctic and boreal ecosystems where mosses drive nitrogen and carbon dynamics in soil layers. His portfolio includes over 130 publications on evolution, genetic variation, and bryophyte biology.

Her role centers on stakeholder engagement, outreach, and implementation of IFAS science rather than peer-reviewed research production. She operates at the practice and adoption layer, delivering science-based guidance to communities, land managers, and local decision-makers. Her work serves local and regional UF/IFAS Extension service areas, community stakeholders, landowners, practitioners, and public audiences engaging with environmental and resource topics.

He develops and applies quantitative social science methods that inform interdisciplinary research, program evaluation, and public health and disaster behavior studies. He also leads BEBR, a major survey and data research center that supports demographic and economic research for policy audiences. His work spans populations studied via egocentric networks (e.g., migrants, health behavior populations), collaborative and scientific research networks, and hard-to-reach and underserved populations. He is the developer of EgoNet, a widely used software tool for personal network data.

Her work sits at the research-to-policy design interface, generating empirical evidence used to evaluate and design energy and environmental regulations. Research systems include electricity markets (U.S. and international), regulated firms and utilities, energy producers and consumers, and policy environments involving emissions pricing, clean energy mandates, and environmental compliance.

She develops and tests models and tools for efficient water and nutrient management, with findings incorporated into Extension fact sheets, irrigation decision tools, and best management practices. Research systems include agricultural landscapes (row crops, specialty crops), urban and managed turf irrigation, and Florida watersheds such as Indian River Lagoon and Everglades inflows.

mossa_joann.research_focus: Dr. Mossa is a physical geographer and geomorphologist with an international reputation for research on fluvial and coastal geomorphology, river channel change, disturbed river systems, river restoration, floodplain dynamics, and hydrologic hazards. She generates mechanistic insights about riverine and coastal systems that inform restoration planning, water resource management, flood hazard mitigation, and environmental regulation. Research systems include large lowland rivers (e.g., Apalachicola, Mississippi, Atchafalaya), floodplain and river-coast interactions in the Gulf Coastal Plain, watershed and channel change under anthropogenic alteration, and river restoration sites (e.g., Kissimmee River). She has published over 100 peer-reviewed publications on rivers, sediment, flow regulation, restoration, and geomorphic responses.

ostebo_marit.title: Associate Professor
education: Ph.D. Anthropology
Research focus on applied anthropology and global health policy
ostebo_marit.research_focus: Dr. Østebø is known for applied and policy-engaged anthropology focused on gender, health, and development, particularly around reproductive health, family planning, harmful traditional practices, and state-society interactions. Her work examines how institutions, policies, and global development agendas intersect with lived experience, often in African contexts. She employs qualitative and ethnographic fieldwork, policy analysis and institutional ethnography, mixed-methods approaches bridging anthropology and public health, and long-term in-depth regional field studies. Research systems include East Africa particularly Ethiopia, health systems NGOs and state institutions, and communities navigating development and public health interventions.

education: Ph.D. Entomology
Research focus on biological control and applied entomology
overholt_william.research_focus: Dr. Overholt is known for his work in applied entomology and biological control, particularly the use of natural enemies to manage invasive insect pests affecting agricultural and natural ecosystems. His research emphasizes classical biological control programs, pest population ecology, and the evaluation of biocontrol agents for sustainable pest management. He employs field and laboratory studies of insect life history, evaluation of parasitoids and predators as biocontrol agents, population monitoring and impact assessment, and collaboration with regulatory and extension systems for deployment. Research systems include agricultural systems in Florida and the southeastern United States, tropical and subtropical ecosystems, and cropping systems and natural areas impacted by invasive pests.

His work has helped clarify how magma generation, differentiation, and eruption processes shape seafloor geology and influence the chemical composition of the oceans over geological timescales. He employs geochemical and isotopic analysis of volcanic rocks, petrologic and mineralogical characterization, field-based marine geology including submersible- and cruise-based sampling, and integration of geochemical data with tectonic and volcanic models. Research systems include mid-ocean ridges and ocean basins, submarine volcanic systems, and global-scale oceanic crust processes.

Research systems include freshwater wetlands and aquatic-terrestrial transition zones, managed and restored wetlands, subtropical systems with global relevance, and strong historical connection to Everglades and similar large wetland systems.

ryan_sadie.research_focus: Dr. Ryan is a leading medical geographer and disease ecologist whose work focuses on quantitative spatial modeling of disease risk, particularly for vector-borne and climate-sensitive diseases, and the social-ecological determinants of disease emergence and spread. She integrates geospatial analysis, ecology, and epidemiology to understand how environmental and climatic factors influence disease dynamics internationally. She employs quantitative geospatial modeling including risk mapping and suitability analyses, climate-health integrative models, interdisciplinary approaches linking ecology epidemiology and geography, and large historical and climate datasets for forecasting disease risk. Research systems include global landscapes of infectious disease risk, tropical and subtropical environments, and human-environment interfaces affecting disease transmission.

Her work combines religious studies, environmental humanities, and cultural analysis to examine moral and ethical dimensions of contemporary environmental and agricultural questions. She employs ethnographic and textual analysis, environmental humanities frameworks, interdisciplinary cultural critique, and analysis of narrative and moral imagination in belief systems and practices. Research systems include cultural and religious systems shaping environmental values, food and agriculture as sites of ethical inquiry, and environmental storytelling and moral frameworks in religion.

His work integrates hydrology, chemistry, and environmental unit operations to improve understanding and management of urban water systems. He employs experimental and field measurements of runoff sediment and contaminant loads, particle dynamics and granulometry in rainfall-runoff, modeling and simulation of hydrodynamic separation and treatment processes, design and evaluation of stormwater best management practices (BMPs), and integration of hydrologic processes with chemical partitioning and treatment efficacy. Research systems include urban and roadway runoff systems, engineered drainage and treatment infrastructure, permeable reactive pavements and eco-infrastructure systems, and watersheds influenced by impervious surfaces and transportation land use.

scheffers_brett.research_focus: Dr. Scheffers is internationally recognized for global-scale conservation biology and climate-change ecology, particularly for showing how climate change, microclimates, habitat structure, and human pressures jointly reshape biodiversity from genes to ecosystems. He is a central figure in demonstrating that fine-scale thermal heterogeneity (microhabitats, forest canopies, soil and near-surface temperatures) can buffer or fail to buffer organisms from climate extremes, with major implications for extinction risk, conservation planning, and human well-being. He employs global meta-analysis and synthesis, large collaborative datasets across multiple taxa and regions, integration of field ecology with climate and spatial data, trait-based and phylogenetic vulnerability analyses, and cross-scale approaches from microhabitats to biomes to people. Research systems include tropical forests especially Southeast Asia and the tropics broadly, global biodiversity patterns across terrestrial systems, and multi-taxon analyses including amphibians reptiles birds mammals and invertebrates.

schumann_arnold.research_focus: Dr. Schumann is internationally known for precision agriculture and sensor-driven nutrient and crop management, with a long and influential body of work on variable-rate nitrogen management, real-time sensing of tree canopy structure, soil-plant variability, and deep learning applications for weed detection, crop health, and yield estimation. He is a foundational figure in translating engineering, sensing, and data analytics into practical nutrient and input management systems, particularly in citrus and perennial cropping systems, with clear implications for productivity, environmental protection, and input efficiency. He employs ultrasonic and sensor-based canopy measurement, variable-rate fertilizer and sprayer systems, deep learning and convolutional neural networks for image analysis, spatial analysis of soil and yield variability, and field-scale experimentation and production-zone delineation. Research systems include citrus groves in Florida and comparable systems, perennial cropping systems, turfgrass blueberry strawberry and row-crop analogs, and commercial agricultural production environments.

Her current research includes analyses of compound flooding (e.g., simultaneous wave runup + storm surge + high tide), atmospheric‐river flood damage trends, and socio-hydrologic risk trajectories for coastal communities. sheremet_alex.research_focus: Dr. Sheremet is known for his fundamental and applied research in coastal and oceanographic engineering, particularly in wave dynamics, nearshore processes, sediment transport, and fluid mechanics in coastal environments. He has contributed to understanding how waves, currents, and wake dynamics interact with seabed sediments and coastal systems, including boat wakes and their environmental impacts, and how these physical processes influence coastal morphology and ecosystem conditions. He employs experimental and theoretical fluid mechanics, numerical and analytical modeling of wave transformation and sediment interactions, time series and spectral analysis of hydrodynamics, field observations and statistical characterizations of wakes and coastal flows, and integration of hydrodynamic observations with coastal response analysis. Research systems include nearshore coastal environments, Florida coastal and intracoastal waters, shelf and surfzone regions where wave and sediment processes interact, and boat-wake influenced waterways and mangrove and shoreline ecotones.

He has made foundational contributions to understanding how water and sediment move across landscapes, how agricultural practices influence runoff and pollutant transport, and how models can be used for watershed planning and decision support. He employs development and application of hydrologic and sediment transport models, field measurement and monitoring of watershed processes, analysis and partitioning of runoff sediment yield and pollutant fluxes, and integration of models with management decision frameworks. Research systems include agricultural watersheds and forested catchments, urbanizing watersheds impacted by land-use change, and Florida and southeastern US landscapes.

sidhu_sudeep.research_focus: Dr. Sidhu is known for his applied agronomic and nutrient management research, focusing on precision nutrient and water management, conservation cropping systems, and best management practices (BMPs) that improve crop productivity while reducing environmental impacts such as nutrient leaching. His work marries field-based agronomy, precision technology, and soil-plant-water processes to generate site-specific strategies for crops like corn, cotton, peanuts, and soybeans. He employs field-based agronomy and long-term demonstration trials, use of lysimeters and deep core soil sampling to assess water quality outcomes, precision tools like Veris MSP3 soil mapping and soil moisture sensors and NDVI for site-specific management, on-farm testing of variable rate irrigation and fertilizer application technologies, and integration of cropping system practices with water and nutrient cycling assessment. Research systems include row crops and forage systems in the Florida Panhandle and Suwannee Valley, soil and water quality implications in sandy humid subtropical soils, and cropping systems with cover crops and rotational grazing.

simmons_cynthia.research_focus: Dr. Simmons is a human geographer whose scholarship examines social-environmental interactions, political economy, land change, and development processes, especially in Amazonia and other Latin American contexts. Her work focuses on how economic development, environmental policy, and social processes at multiple scales influence local landscapes, livelihoods, and conflicts over land and natural resources. She employs qualitative and quantitative social science methods, political economy and interdisciplinary analysis, fieldwork and case studies in Latin America, and critical geography approaches to environment-society relations. Research systems include Amazonian frontier regions and the broader Amazon basin, Latin American rural and agrarian landscapes, and human-environment interaction zones where policy, economy, and ecology intersect.

He employs multi-scale optical reflectance spectroscopy, proximal and airborne imaging spectroscopy, integration of remote sensing with mechanistic process models, and development of remote sensing-derived products for stakeholders. Research systems include forest and grassland ecosystem structure and function, agricultural ecosystems with optical stress detection for irrigation demand and nutrient use, and landscape-scale nutrient and water stress assessment.

He employs epidemiological assessment of progression and spatial distribution of plant diseases, integration of remote sensing image analysis machine learning and sensor data for disease detection and modeling, development of decision tools and algorithms for risk assessment in crop protection, and interdisciplinary collaboration with agronomists breeders engineers and computer scientists. Research systems include South Florida coastal wetlands and estuaries, oyster reefs salt marshes seagrass meadows and urban coastal waters, and broader subtropical and estuarine ecosystems undergoing climate and anthropogenic pressures.

Research systems include unsaturated soils and porous geomaterials and three-phase porous media, slope stability and geohazard contexts influenced by environmental loads, and computational frameworks applicable to infrastructure resilience in variable environmental settings.

stofer_kathryn.research_focus: Dr. Stofer is known for her scholarship in science communication, public engagement with science, and informal science education, particularly at the intersection of agriculture, environment, and emerging technologies. She employs qualitative and quantitative social science research, content analysis of media and science messaging, survey research on public attitudes and trust, case studies of science outreach extension and engagement programs, and interdisciplinary collaboration with scientists and communicators. subalusky_amanda.research_focus: Dr. Subalusky is an ecosystem ecologist best known for her work on cross-ecosystem nutrient fluxes, consumer-driven biogeochemistry, and the ecological consequences of large animal populations. Her research has been especially influential in demonstrating how large vertebrates such as hippopotamus act as mobile nutrient vectors, fundamentally reshaping aquatic-terrestrial linkages, food webs, and biogeochemical cycles. She combines field ecology with biogeochemical measurements to reveal how animal behavior and density scale up to ecosystem-level processes. She employs field experiments and long-term ecological monitoring, measurement of nutrient fluxes including nitrogen phosphorus and carbon, stable isotope analysis and ecosystem mass balance approaches, comparative studies across gradients of animal density, and integration of ecological theory with biogeochemical data. Research systems include tropical river and savanna ecosystems notably East Africa, freshwater rivers influenced by large herbivores, aquatic-terrestrial interface zones, and broader relevance to global change and defaunation scenarios.

swain_banikalyan.research_focus: Dr. Swain is known for his veterinary and comparative immunology research, especially in molecular mechanisms of host immune responses and innovative vaccine development for aquaculture pathogens using recombinant vaccine vectors. His work focuses on designing and evaluating genetically engineered microbes that stimulate protective immunity in fish, with broader implications for animal health and infectious disease control. He employs molecular cloning and genetic engineering, recombinant vaccine construction and evaluation, immunological assays including antibody response and cytokine profiling, fish disease challenge models for aquatic infection studies, cellular and molecular immunology techniques, and integration of host immunobiology with pathogen genomics. Research systems include aquatic vertebrates especially teleost fish and their infectious disease agents, Edwardsiella piscicida and other bacterial fish pathogens, and comparative immunological systems relevant to broader veterinary infectious disease.

swisher_mickie.research_focus: Dr. Swisher is known for her interdisciplinary work in sustainable agriculture, community food systems, and decision-making under risk and uncertainty, particularly as they relate to farmers, rural communities, and food security. She bridges social science, sustainable farming systems, and collaborative research design to assess how individuals and communities make complex agricultural decisions and how community systems adapt to environmental, economic, and social changes. She employs mixed-methods social science research including qualitative and quantitative approaches, participatory and stakeholder-driven research design, community assessment and program evaluation tools, and collaborative interdisciplinary approaches combining social science with biological and environmental research. Research systems include sustainable and organic food systems in Florida and beyond, rural development and food systems in Latin America the Caribbean and Africa, and community food security farmer decision-making and agricultural practice adoption.

taylor_bron.research_focus: Dr. Taylor is one of the world's leading scholars at the intersection of religion, environmental ethics, and ecology, renowned for pioneering work on "dark green religion" - a theoretical framework describing spiritually grounded environmental ethics with deep reverence for nature. His scholarship blends religious studies, environmental philosophy, and social science to investigate how beliefs and spiritual narratives shape environmental values, ethical obligations, and grassroots environmental movements worldwide. He employs interdisciplinary synthesis combining ethnography, historical analysis, philosophical and ethical inquiry, and cultural interpretation, as well as qualitative and text-based analysis of environmental movements, mythologies, and cultural narratives. Research systems include human-environment belief systems worldwide, and the spiritual and ethical dimensions of conservation and sustainability discourse.

subject_areas: Nitrogen fixation and plant growth-promoting microbes
Human and animal microbiomes including early-life immune development

education: Ph.D. in Microbiology
Research focus on molecular microbial ecology and microbiome science
triplett_eric.research_focus: Dr. Triplett is a foundational figure in molecular microbial ecology. He is widely known for pioneering high-throughput, culture-independent methods to characterize microbial diversity and for applying these tools to soil, rhizosphere, freshwater, and host-associated microbiomes. His work helped establish how environmental factors such as soil pH, land use, and plant hosts structure microbial communities and how microbes, in turn, influence nutrient cycling, plant productivity, and host health. He has also made major contributions to plant-microbe interactions including biological nitrogen fixation and to human microbiome research related to immune development and autoimmunity. He employs high-throughput sequencing including 16S rRNA and metagenomics, development and application of microbial diversity metrics, comparative microbial community analysis across environments, genomics of bacteria and microbial consortia, and integration of molecular data with environmental and host metadata. Research systems include agricultural soils and rhizospheres, natural and managed freshwater systems, tropical and temperate soils including deforestation gradients, and host-associated systems such as plant roots and human gut microbiomes.

subject_areas: Phylogeography and population structure of pathogens
Horizontal gene transfer and pathogen evolution

education: Ph.D. in Microbiology/Molecular Biology

tuanyok_apichai.research_focus: Dr. Tuanyok is an internationally recognized medical and environmental microbiologist best known for his work on Burkholderia pseudomallei and Burkholderia mallei, the causative agents of melioidosis and glanders. His research centers on bacterial genomics, phylogeography, virulence evolution, and environmental reservoirs, with major contributions to understanding how these pathogens persist in soil and water, spread geographically, evolve via horizontal gene transfer, and cause human and animal disease. He is a leading authority on the genomic epidemiology and environmental biology of these high-consequence pathogens. He employs whole-genome sequencing and comparative genomics, phylogenetic and phylogeographic reconstruction, analysis of lateral gene transfer and genomic islands, transcriptomics and gene expression analysis, environmental sampling frameworks for pathogen detection, and integration of genomic data with clinical and environmental metadata. Research systems include soil and environmental reservoirs of Burkholderia pseudomallei, tropical and subtropical regions such as Southeast Asia and Australia, and clinical and environmental isolates from human animal and environmental sources.

tucker_catherine.research_focus: Dr. Tucker is an interdisciplinary social-ecological systems scholar and cultural and environmental anthropologist whose work explores environmental governance, collective action, common-pool resource management, climate adaptation, and sustainability, especially in mountainous and rural Latin American contexts. Her research integrates institutional analysis, political ecology, and ethnography to understand how communities interact with their environments and make decisions about natural resource use under socioeconomic and climatic stress. She employs mixed qualitative and quantitative social science research, ethnographic fieldwork and participant observation, institutional and policy analysis, and engagement with interdisciplinary teams on social-ecological modeling and governance. Geographic expertise includes Central America such as Honduras and Guatemala, Mexico, Peru, and broader comparative mountain regions.

unruh_j.research_focus: Dr. Unruh is known for his leadership in turfgrass science and management, focusing on nutrient and water management, environmental stewardship, and sustainable turfgrass systems. He is a recognized authority on best management practices (BMPs) that reduce nutrient leaching and runoff from turfgrass landscapes, improve water-use efficiency, and promote environmentally responsible maintenance in golf courses, sod production, and urban landscapes. He employs field experimentation on turf performance irrigation and fertilization regimes, assessment of nutrient dynamics including nitrogen leaching studies in managed landscapes, development and evaluation of cultivar responses to environmental stresses, and collaboration with extension and industry stakeholders to translate research to practice. Research systems include turfgrass systems in Florida's subtropical and Gulf Coast climates, golf courses athletic fields urban lawns and sod production environments, and water-limited and nutrient concern landscapes where stewardship practices matter.

vallelevinson_arnoldo.research_focus: Dr. Valle-Levinson is a leading coastal physical oceanographer and hydrodynamic engineer whose research focuses on estuarine and coastal circulation, water exchange processes, tidal and subtidal flows, and coastal ocean dynamics. He integrates field observations, analytical theory, and numerical modeling to understand how waters move within and between estuaries, bays, fjords, lagoons, and the adjacent coastal ocean, including effects of wind, tides, density gradients, bathymetry, and climate variability. He employs field measurements with acoustic Doppler current profilers (ADCPs) and hydrographic instruments, analytical and numerical modeling of fluid mechanics in estuaries and coastal basins, long-term observational analyses of water level and flow variability, and synthesis of dynamics across climatic geographic and hydrologic gradients. Research systems include estuaries semi-enclosed coastal basins and shelves in temperate subtropical and tropical regions, Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic coastal waters in context of sea-level variability and estuarine exchange, and comparative studies spanning fjords lagoons and straits across global settings.

He employs gas exchange and chlorophyll fluorescence for photosynthetic analysis, physiological measurements of water relations carbon allocation and tree hydraulics, field and controlled environment studies of crop responses to management and stress, and applied plant science integrated with cropping system factors. His work integrates ecological, biogeochemical, and management perspectives to understand how forests maintain productivity, nutrient balance, and structural complexity under environmental change and human intervention. He employs field ecological measurements including growth biomass and nutrient pools in forests, nutrient and carbon balance analyses across forest gradients and treatments, long-term ecological and silvicultural experiments, and integration of process-based ecological understanding with forest management scenarios. wade_tara.research_focus: Dr. Wade is a natural resource and agricultural economist specializing in empirical analysis of conservation practice adoption, resource decisions by farmers and landowners, and the economic incentives shaping environmental stewardship and ecosystem service provision. Her work blends econometric and discrete choice models with large datasets to understand why producers adopt or do not adopt conservation-oriented practices and how policy and market incentives can be designed to improve environmental outcomes. She employs econometrics including logit and choice models using large administrative and survey datasets, applied quantitative modeling of adoption decisions and policy response, and production of extension economics tools such as enterprise budgets and price trend analyses. Research systems include agricultural production systems in Florida and the U.S., specialty crop economics such as bell peppers watermelons and sweet corn, and economic dimensions of soil water and carbon markets.

waylen_peter.research_focus: Dr. Waylen is a climatologist and physical geographer best known for his work on climate variability, hydroclimatology, and climate-society linkages, with a strong emphasis on regional climate patterns, rainfall variability, extremes, and teleconnections such as ENSO. His scholarship connects atmospheric processes to hydrologic responses and human systems, particularly in the Caribbean, Central America, Florida, and other tropical-subtropical regions. Research systems include the Caribbean and Central America, Florida and the southeastern United States, tropical and subtropical river basins, and regions sensitive to climate variability and extremes.

wells_jamin.research_focus: Dr. Wells is a historian of environment, science, and technology whose work examines how landscapes, infrastructure, and environmental knowledge are shaped by political, economic, and cultural forces. He is particularly known for scholarship on the environmental and infrastructural history of the modern United States, including water management, conservation, and the long-term consequences of development and governance decisions on ecological systems. He employs archival research and historical analysis, interpretation of policy documents technical reports and institutional records, and interdisciplinary synthesis linking historical narratives to environmental systems. Research systems include the United States with emphasis on modern and contemporary periods, landscapes shaped by infrastructure including water control land use and conservation regimes, and institutions governing environmental knowledge and practice.

wen_yixin.research_focus: Dr. Wen is a human-environment geographer whose work focuses on land use and land-cover change, environmental governance, and spatial patterns of sustainability and development. Her research examines how human decision-making, institutions, and socioeconomic forces shape landscapes over time, often using spatial and quantitative methods to understand environmental change, resource use, and policy impacts. She employs Geographic Information Systems and remote sensing, spatial analysis and quantitative modeling of land-use dynamics, and integration of spatial data with socioeconomic and policy variables. Research systems include urban and peri-urban landscapes, regions experiencing rapid land-use change, and comparative geographic analyses in U.S. and international contexts.

His research applies rigorous quantitative methods to evaluate how policies, prices, and shocks influence outcomes in agricultural and resource systems. He employs econometric modeling including panel data causal inference and structural models, analysis of large administrative and survey datasets, quantitative policy evaluation and counterfactual analysis, and integration of economic models with environmental and production contexts. Research systems include U.S. agricultural systems with relevance to Florida specialty crops, market and policy environments affecting producers and land managers, and resource and environmental policy contexts influencing agricultural decisions.

whiles_matthew.research_focus: Dr. Whiles is a stream and aquatic ecologist internationally recognized for his work on energy flow, nutrient dynamics, and food-web structure in streams and rivers. He is especially known for integrating benthic ecology, invertebrate production, organic matter dynamics, and biogeochemical processes to understand how freshwater ecosystems function across land-water interfaces. His research clarifies how nutrients and energy move through aquatic systems and how disturbances such as land use change and hydrologic alteration affect ecosystem processes. He employs field measurements of nutrient fluxes organic matter and invertebrate production, stable isotope analysis to trace energy and nutrient pathways, quantification of secondary production and trophic structure, long-term and comparative field studies across watersheds, and integration of ecological and biogeochemical datasets. Research systems include streams and rivers in agricultural and forested landscapes, midwestern and southeastern U.S. freshwater systems, watersheds experiencing land-use change or nutrient loading, and comparative studies across temperate freshwater ecosystems.

Her research integrates applied feeding trials with physiological and biochemical measurements to evaluate how diet composition, nutrient timing, and supplementation affect outcomes such as gut health, energy metabolism, and welfare. She employs controlled feeding and supplementation trials, physiological and metabolic measurements including blood metabolites and digestion markers, diet formulation and nutrient balance assessment, and integration of animal performance metrics with nutritional inputs. Research systems include horses as the primary emphasis, livestock production systems relevant to Florida and the southeastern U.S., and managed animal systems where nutrition directly affects health and productivity.

wilson_patrick.research_focus: Dr. Wilson is an environmental engineer known for his work on water quality, contaminant fate and transport, and treatment technologies in agricultural and urban water systems. He employs field and laboratory measurements of water and soil quality, design and evaluation of treatment wetlands and best management practices, nutrient mass balance and transport analysis, monitoring of surface and groundwater systems, and applied engineering analysis for real-world systems. yoon_seonkyoo.research_focus: Dr. Yoon is a physical hydrologist whose research investigates the dynamics of fluid flow and reactive transport in geological media—particularly porous and fractured rock systems. [oai_citation:1‡geology.ufl.edu](https://geology.ufl.edu/directory/dr-seonkyoo-yoon/?utm_source=chatgpt.com) His work blends theoretical, computational, experimental and field-based approaches to understand how subsurface structure, flow inertia, diffusion, mixing, and geochemical reactions influence transport of water, solutes and energy across scales. zhao_chang.research_focus: Dr. Zhao's work centers on Geographic Information Science, Remote Sensing, Geovisualization, Ecosystem Service Assessments, and AI (Machine Learning and Deep Learning). Her current projects involve quantifying ecosystem service dynamics across urban and agricultural landscapes using machine learning and remote sensing, integrating socioeconomic data to understand drivers affecting human well-being.

zimmerman_andrew.name: Andrew Zimmerman
zimmerman_andrew.title: Associate Professor

subject_areas: Carbon cycling and organic matter dynamics

education: Ph.D. in Geochemistry/Earth Sciences
Research focus on carbon cycling and organic matter transformations
zimmerman_andrew.research_focus: Dr. Zimmerman is an environmental geochemist recognized for his work on carbon cycling, organic matter transformations, and biogeochemical processes in soils and sediments. His research focuses on how carbon is stabilized, transformed, and exchanged between terrestrial and aquatic systems, with implications for climate feedbacks, land-use change, and long-term carbon storage. He employs stable isotope analysis including carbon isotopes, molecular and bulk characterization of organic matter, laboratory and field studies of soils and sediments, experimental manipulation of biogeochemical conditions, and integration of geochemical data with ecosystem processes. Research systems include soils and sediments across terrestrial and aquatic environments, systems influenced by land-use change and disturbance, and interfaces between rivers wetlands and coastal environments.

zotarelli_lincoln.name: Lincoln Zotarelli
zotarelli_lincoln.title: Professor

subject_areas: Nitrogen management and fertilizer use efficiency
Soil moisture dynamics in sandy soils
Soil organic matter and aggregate-associated carbon

education: Ph.D. in Soil Science/Horticulture
Research focus on irrigation and nutrient management
zotarelli_lincoln.research_focus: Dr. Zotarelli is an internationally recognized horticultural scientist known for his work on irrigation management, nitrogen and water use efficiency, and soil-plant-water interactions in vegetable cropping systems, particularly on sandy soils. He is especially well known for linking irrigation scheduling, nutrient management, and soil physical processes to improve crop productivity while reducing nutrient losses and environmental impacts. He employs field experiments in commercial and research-scale cropping systems, soil moisture sensing and interpretation, nitrogen mass balance and leaching measurements, evapotranspiration modeling using FAO-56 Penman-Monteith, integration of irrigation technology with nutrient management, and applied modeling and decision-support tools. Research systems include vegetable cropping systems in Florida and subtropical regions, sandy and low-organic-matter soils, drip irrigation seepage irrigation and plasticulture systems, and comparative systems including tropical and subtropical agroecosystems.

vonmeding_jason.name: Jason von Meding
vonmeding_jason.role: Associate Professor, Rinker School of Construction Management; Founding Faculty Member, Florida Institute for Built Environment Resilience (FIBER)
vonmeding_jason.academic_unit: M.E. Rinker Sr. School of Construction Management & FIBER, University of Florida
vonmeding_jason.research_focus: Dr. Jason von Meding studies the root causes of disaster risk by examining how built environments, social inequity, governance structures and community vulnerabilities intersect. His research integrates architecture and construction management with disaster studies, emphasizing community-centered, participatory approaches to resilience and built-environment adaptation. He examines how disasters reveal systemic injustices, and how sustainable and equitable recovery processes can be designed and led by the communities most impacted.
